summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had been feeling short of breath and you were found to have fluid on your lungs. This was felt to be due to a condition called heart failure, where your heart does not pump hard enough and fluid backs up into your lungs. You were given a diuretic medication through the IV to help get the fluid out. You also had a procedure called an ablation that will help keep you out of your irregular heart rhythm. You were also found to have low thyroid levels and were started on a medication to replace the thyroid hormone. Your blood sugar was found to be elevated Your weight at discharge is 194Lbs. Please weigh yourself today at home and use this as your new baseline. Please weigh yourself every day in the morning.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with a history of HTN and pulmonary HTN who presents with SOB and hypoxia. . # Hypoxia, SOB: CHF versus pneumonia were on differentials. Pneumonia was considered with pt's history of progressively worsening cough and shortness of breath with leukocytosis and subjective fevers. On hospital Day 2, pt was febrile to ___ and was started on Levaquin. CXR shows pleural effusions but no lobar consolidations, and given that pt recently stopped diuretics, it was likely pt was fluid overloaded. This was supported by pt's elevated BNP. TTE showed moderate mitral regurgitation and pulmonary hyptertension (stable from prior). Pt was diuresed with Lasix and given captopril for BP control but was discontinued after UOP decreased and creatinine increased. Pt did well with antibiotic and her oxygen supplementation was weaned. She was discharged on levofloxacin to complete a 7-day course, as well as furosemide 10mg daily until she sees her PCP ___ ___. . # HTN: Pt was on felodipine, atenolol and Losartan as an outpatient. Pt came in with SBP in 190s and we continued felodipine and started TID captopril. On hospital day 2 pt became oliguric and Cr bump to 1.3 and thus stopped captopril. SBP in 130s and we restarted home atenolol. Once ___ resolved, all home antihypertensive medications were resumed, as well as furosemide as noted above. . # Anxiety: Pt was having multiple anxiety episodes upon admission and we continued her home diazepam. During these episodes, pt's BP increased significantly with tachycardia and had periods of nausea. Pt received valium at night and an ativan when she was particularly anxious. # Hyponatremia: Pt's sodium remained low throughout MICU course and has a history of having diuretics changed due to hyponatremia. Urine lytes, osm and urea were checked which showed low Na and osm consistent with dehyradation. Pt was hydrated and hypoNa resolved. # Anemia: Pt has h/o of anemia which had been worked up recently with EGD and colonoscopy. Would recommend outpatient follow-up given that her Fe was 7 and TIBC was low during admission. . # Hypothyroidism: Chronic and stable. We continued Synthroid at home dose. . # Renal lesions: Two renal lesions were seen incidentally on CT. Recommend that PCP get renal ultrasound to further characterize and to eval for renal cell carcinoma (considering her iron deficiency anemia of unclear etiology). . # Transitional Issues: - Needs renal ultrasound - Anemia workup
summary: You were admitted because you have been having shortness of breath and fevers. The chest x-ray and cat scan show that you have pneumonia as well as fluid in your lungs which is likely related to the pneumonia. You also have pulmonary hypertension which has been a longstanding issue, but which may be contributing. We are treating you with an antibiotic called levofloxacin and restarted lasix (a diuretic) to remove the fluid from your lungs until you see your primary care doctor and/or cardiologist at which time they may decide to stop this. You are anemic which is not new, but which is slightly worse than prior, and you were found to have low iron. Your primary care doctor ___ need to do further workup for this. The cat scan showed two lesions in your kidneys which may be benign cysts, however your primary care doctor ___ need to do further workup for this.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ with PMH significant for severe valvular heart disease, diastolic CHF (EF 55%) with pacemaker, CABG x 5, Afib not on anticoagulation, heart block s/p PPM, DM2, and dementia who presented to ___ on ___ after an unwitnessed fall and was found to have broken ribs. # Goals of Care: Mr. ___ daughter, ___, and son, ___, met with the team regarding goals of care for Mr. ___. His current clinical status was discussed, including evidence of poor perfusion on labs and on exam. The prognosis of both his acute fractures/injuries as well as his longstanding underlying, irreversible heart disease were described to the best of our knowledge, and the family demonstrated understanding. ___ elected to pursue comfort-centered care, focusing on symptom based treatment. Regular scheduled labs, disruptive vitals, and telemetry were discontinued (temperature and respiratory rate were continued). His DNR/DNI/Do not escalate/Do not transfer to ICU status was confirmed by both of his children. Plans were made to transfer to a long-term care facility with hospice care. Any treatments that could help his symptoms should be considered, including antibiotics to treat symptomatic infections (such as fever or rigors not controlled by tylenol). # Unwitnessed fall: CT head at OSH was negative for intracranial bleed. CT neck did not show dispalced fracture and the Acute Care Surgical Team cleared his C spine prior to transfer to cardiology. CT chest and abdomen showed multiple Rt rib fractures (___), moderate and small left nonhemorrhagic effusions and intra-abdominal ascites. The patient was admitted to the TICU for observation and pain management and transferred to the Advanced Heart Failure Unit once hemodynamic stability was verified. # Rib fractures: Right ___ rib fractures, including one displaced fracture, seen on CT. Pain management with PO tylenol ___ IV q8h, Lidocaine patches, Hydromorphone ___ PO q4H PRN pain or dyspnea. Incentive Spirometry as able to tolerate without worsening pain was encouraged, to prevent atelectasis and decrease risk of pneumonia. He had a new O2 requirement, likely due to shallow breathing/splinting. # Heart failure/valvular disease: Mr. ___ was not fluid overloaded, likely due to decreased PO intake over the previous weeks. If he become fluid overloaded, he can take his previous dose of torsemide (30mg three times a week), on an as-needed basis for symptomatic relief of dyspnea. # Decreased PO intake: Likely due to decreased appetite and possible metabolic requirement changes consistent with severe illness. He was seen and evaluated by speech pathology, who found no swallowing problems and was cleared for whole pills and a regular diet with thin liquids. His regular meals were supplemented with ensure shakes while he was an inpatient. # Dementia: According to discussion with daughter, he is usually oriented to self and sometimes place. Appears to be near baseline. # Chronic medical conditions: Medications given without the effect of symptomatic relief were discontinued. This includes metoprolol, vitamin supplementation, and Simvastatin. Scheduled fingerstick blood glucoses were discontinued, as well as sliding-scale insulin. If he appears hypoglycemic (weak, shaking, or decreased consciousness) PRN FSBG could be checked for the goal of managing these symptoms.
summary: You were transferred here for care after a fall, which resulted in multiple rib fractures. Initially you were assessed by the Acute Care Surgery team, who transferred you to the Advanced Heart Failure Unit after you were deemed to be stable. Given the severity of your underlying heart disease and the acute stress of injuries resulting from the fall, the palliative care team came to speak with you and your family about ways to optimize pain control and other symptoms. It was decided to orient your medical care to maximize comfort, and to transfer you to the long-term care facility at ___ with hospice care.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ with history of CAD s/p 4v-CABG, AFIB s/p PPM placement, HFpEF (EF 45-50%), lupus anticoagulant with history of venous thromboembolism (previously on dabigatran, now on warfarin and vitamin K daily), myasthenia ___ (requiring IVIG, high dose prednisone, MMF), COPD, and ITP s/p splenectomy, who presented with failure to thrive and weakness, desiring further IVIG. ACUTE ISSUES: #WEAKNESS + FAILURE TO THRIVE: #MYASTHENIA ___: Patient presents with weakness and failure to thrive. This was originally suspected a worsening of his myasthenia ___ especially in the setting of recent prednisone taper, though the differential could also include underlying infection. Patient has known anemia which is stable to improved from prior. No fevers or leukocytosis to point to specific infectious etiology. No ongoing localizing symptoms to suggest ongoing C. difficile, UTI or other infectious source. Other potential etiology for weakness could be mild HFrEF exacerbation, detailed further below. Neurology was consulted and did not determine a significant change in muscle strength relative to prior exam. Thus determined administration of IVIG would pose a greater risk given CHF and fluid status than potential long term benefit. Palliative care consulted, know this pt well and discussed goals of care which remained oriented towards home hospice. Patient was adamant that if he was not to get IVIG inpatient, he would like to return home. Occasionally found to collapse into hyper-somnolent states per son with thorough workup negative for a known etiology. Prednisone was increased to help alleviate MG burden and somnolence. Less weight placed on long term risks for infections. Completing 5 day course of PO vanc prophylaxis given abx initiated transiently. #Acute on chronic Heart failure with systolic dysfunction: Patient w/ mild HFrEF w/ ECHO in ___ showed EF of 40%. Patient presents with CXR with mild pulmonary edema and BNP 6676 from recent prior of >12,000. Clinical appearance including ___ edema could represent mild exacerbation. Unable to identify clear dry weight from prior admission, current 77 kgs which is substantially below prior admission weight. CXR on ___ significant for pulmonary edema and enlarge pleural effusion. Pitting edema and elevated JVD remaining. Continued home spironolactone and continued diuresis with Lasix 80mg IV BID with transition to home torsemide. #Atrial fibrillation s/p PPM + Lupus Anticoagulant w/ Hx of deep venous thrombosis: On warfarin at home, was dosed warfarin daily: ___ as of ___, given pt refused ___. Continued home metoprolol and phytonadione 100 mcg daily. CHRONIC ISSUES: #coronary artery disease s/p 4v-CABG: Continued home aspirin 81mg daily, home simvastatin #Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease: Continued home albuterol nebs, home ipratroprium nebs #Gastroesophageal reflux disease: Continued home pantoprazole #GOC + PAIN MANAGEMENT: Patient was discharged home on hospice and readmitted with a dilaudid infusion pump SQ in place. This was discontinued overnight and patient started on bolus IV dilaudid dosing. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== -Follow-up: PCP and neurology -___ medications: Prednisone was increased from 10 mg to 40 mg -Pain: Please continue dilaudid at home if he is in pain as patient is having hospice care. Patient did not require dilaudid ___ days prior to discharge. -Prednisone was started to decrease somnolence. Please downtitrate as needed as an outpatient. He will follow-up with neurology, who did not feel this was a clear myasthenia exacerbation. -Continue ___ discussions as an outpatient especially regarding hospital admissions. -Warfarin: He will go home on 3 mg warfarin and will need daily INR checks until therapeutic for history of AF and lupus anticoagulant. -Code status: DNR/DNI -Contact: ___ (son) ___ Greater than 30 minutes spent in care coordination and counseling on the day of discharge.
summary: You were hospitalized because of concern for a myasthenia ___ crisis which may have precipitated. You were also found to be fluid overloaded from your heart failure disease. Pictures were taken that showed that you did not have an acute bleed or stroke in your brain. You also were found to have some congestion in your lungs which may have been caused from your heart disease. You were started on medications to help remove extra fluid from your body to alleviate the burden on your heart and lungs. You were increased on medications to help reduce your autoimmune disease and assist with your myasthenia ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ======== Summary: ======== ___ year-old man with PMH significant for COPD, EtOH abuse (18+ beers daily, recently decreased to 8+) polysubstance abuse (last IVDU in ___, and HCV (reports resolved without treatment) but no other known active liver disease, presents with new ascites and hypervolemia. ============ ACUTE ISSUES: ============ # Volume overload: Patient presented with new onset bilateral lower extremity edema and small-moderate ascites. Initial differential included cirrhosis, CHF, versus nephrotic syndrome. With regards to CHF, BNP was slightly elevated to 400, EKG without abnormalities, and CXR without cardiomegaly or pulmonary edema. With regards to nephrotic syndrome, UA with only trace protein. RUQ ultrasound did not demonstrate evidence of cirrhosis but albumin low, INR elevated to 1.3, and history of both ETOH and prior HCV. Ascites protein of 1.7 suggestive of cirrhosis. Additionally, diagnostic peritoneal tap performed that showed no evidence of SBP and no encephalopathy on exam. Hepatology consulted who agree with most likely cirrhosis as etiology. Patient diuresed with 20mg IV lasix and discharged on oral lasix and aldactone with follow up with ___. =============== CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # ETOH Abuse: Patient was drinking 18+ beers/day then decreased to about 8 per day. EtOH level negative on admission. LFTs consistent with alcohol liver disease, MCV elevated to 104. Last drink ___ prior to admission. Patient maintained on CIWA scale with evidence of alcohol withdrawal during hospital stay. Patient started on Thiamine, folate, MVI. Met with social work regarding alcohol use and planned to call ___ himself for an appointment after discharge. # Malnutrition: Albumin 2.7 and INR elevated to 1.3 on admission. Per nutrition, patient with moderate malnutrition. Encouraged lean protein with small frequent meals and multivitamins per above. # COPD: Current smoker with no evidence of exacerbation on exam. Was continued on home Albuterol Inhaler 2 PUFF IH Q4H:PRN SOB, wheezing and home Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus (250/50) 1 INH IH BID # GERD: Continued on Omeprazole 20 mg PO DAILY ==================== Transitional Issues: ==================== - Patient started on Lasix 40mg and Spironolactone 50mg daily for diuresis given fluid overload and ascites. - Patient requested to obtain outpatient lab work with chemistry panel (Na, K, Cl, CO2, BUN, Cr, glucose, Mg, Phos, Ca), CBC, and LFTs (AST, ALT, ALP, Tbili, albumin) on ___ and faxed to Dr. ___. - Please continue outpatient counseling for alcohol abstinence. - Nutrition recommended lean protein with small frequent meals. - Please ensure PCP and ___. - Please ___ pending blood and peritoneal fluid cultures from ___. - Please ___ additional liver studies, including Hepatitis B, Hepatitis C, ___, AMA, and smooth muscle. - Please ___ urine albumin for evaluation of his volume overload. Your liver disease is likely related to alcohol use but additional studies were sent and are pending. You were seen by the Liver Team as well and will see them again after you leave the hospital. You were also seen by Nutrition who recommended that you eat lean protein with small frequent meals. It is extremely important that you cut back on your alcohol intake. Please discuss with your primary care doctors options to help you quit alcohol. Stopping drinking will help to prevent further progression of your liver disease. You were started on new medications called furosemide (Lasix) and spironolactone to help remove this extra fluid from your body. Your discharge weight is 59.8kg or 131.56lbs.

summary: You were admitted for workup of heart failure and respiratory problems. You underwent a coronary catheterization to assess for heart artery disease. That procedure was complicated by sudden worsening of your respiratory problems which required you to be admitted to the CCU (cardiac intensive care unit) for intensive treatment. Your respiratory problems were largely a result of fluid overload from heart failure. We gave you medicines to make you urinate (diuretics) and removed this excess fluid. You are being discharged on a maintenance dose of these diuretics. While in the hospital, you underwent cardiac MRI to investigate your heart. It appears that your heart failure may be due to hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with h/o COPD on O2, lung cancer s/p left pneumonectomy and chemoradiation (___), HTN, and HLD admitted initially to ICU with acute respiratory failure requiring COPD exacerbation from pneumonia resulting in demand NSTEMI, stress cardiomyopathy, and HFrEF, course complicated by R. femoral CVL-associated DVT and persistent desaturations. #Acute hypoxemic respiratory failure #COPD exacerbation # RLL CAP Patient s/p L pneumonectomy for lung Ca, thus with reduced reserve. Eventually she was extubated and slowly was weaned from daytime oxygen, at the time of discharge mostly just requiring nighttime oxygen as before. She will continue to track her oxygen saturation and use exertional oxygen if needed. She was treated with ceftriaxone and doxycycline for total 5d CAP treatment, day 1 = ___. summary: It was a priviliege to care for you at the ___ ___. You were admitted for difficulty breathing that ultimately required temporary placement of a breathing tube and mechanical ventilation. We suspect this was caused by a variety of different factors including a COPD exacerbation, underlying pneumonia, and stress on your heart that has reduced it's ability to pump blood. All of these factors are particularly challenging for a patient with only one lung. You received a variety of treatments including steroids, nebulizers, chest physical therapy, and new medications for your heart including lasix.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M with h/o COPD, post-op afib, recent 1 month rehab stay presented with infiltrate on CXR, leukocytosis, productive cough, pleuritic chest pain and shortness of breath c/w health-care assoc pneumonia. Pneumonia: Patient was admitted for shortness of breath, productive cough, chest pain, fever and leukocytosis (WBC 12.8) consistent with health care associated pneumonia (given recent surgery and stay in nursing home). He was treated empirically with vancomycin and Levofloxacin with some clinical improvement. Pt was instructed to continue Levofloxacin for a total of 7 days. Given his history of COPD patient's home regimen of Spiriva 18 mcg daily, Advair 250/50 mcg bid Albuterol MDI 2 puff Singulair 10 daily was continued. Chest xray on admission ___ showed subtle R subhilar infiltrate and bilateral pleural effusions. Repeat chest xray on ___ showed no change. White count started to trend down from 13.6 to 12.0 at discharge. . Pulmonary edema/Acute diastolic CHF: On admission, bilateral lower lobe inspiratory crackles were noted. The patient also complained of orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, and dyspnea exertion. A one time bolus of 10mg IV Lasix was administered on ___, and patient reported drastic improvement the above symptoms. He was further diuresed, and an ECHO was obtained and showed normal LVEF and moderate pulmonary artery systolic hypertension likely due to underlying COPD. It was thought that he likely developped volume overload in setting of post op AFib with RVR and diastolic dysfunction. Pt will need ongoing instructions regarding monitoring his weight daily and if he notes a weight gain of greater than 3lbs, 20mg PO Lasix should be administered for diuresis. . Pt was scheduled to follow up with his cardiologist at ___ for Afib and newly diagnosed diastolic heart failure. Chest Pain: Patient was admitted with pleuritic chest pain. The workup revealed a normal ECG, and normal cardiac biomarkers x 3. His pain resolved one day after admission. Hx of Afib: Prior to admission pt developed Afib in the post-op setting and converted to sinus with amiodarone. Patient was on home Amiodorone 200mg BID. We continued amiodarone, and reduced the dose to 200mg daily per instruction from patient's cardiologist Dr. ___. HR remained in the ___ throughout admission without any evidence of RVR. Hx of COPD: Pt has a long standing history of COPD and his home medicines were continued including singulair, advair, spiriva and albuterol. Chronic kidney disease: baseline of 1.3. Patient was monitored closely for changes in renal function, and on discharge creatinine was 1.4.
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath, cough and fever. You were treated for pneumonia. You also had some fluid in your lungs. You were treated with a diuretic in order to remove the water from your lungs. You had an echocardiogram that showed normal heart function. It also showed evidence of high pressures in the lungs- pulmonary hypertension, that will need to be followed up by your primary care doctor. We changed some of your medications. We have added an antibiotic "Levofloxacin". You will continue taking this medication for another day until ___. We have also changed the dose of Amiodorone to 200mg once daily, per recommendation of your cardiologist Dr. ___. We have decreased your simvastatin to 20mg because it can interact with amiodarone and cause muscle damage at higher doses. We have stopped ambien, as you were able to sleep better once we gave you medication to remove water from the lungs. All of your other medications remain the same.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Pt is a ___ w/long complicated PMH including AF, thrombosed Aortic dissection w/complicated surgical course, DMII, with afib w/RVR and chronic diastolic CHF, comes from rehab nursing home with with DOE and tachycardia and found to have afib w/RVR and worsening CHF symptoms. summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were experiencing exacerbation of your underlying heart conditions. It was found that you were fluid overloaded and that your heart was beating in an irregular pattern. You were given medications to help remove the excess fluid in your body that had accumulated due to your underlying chronic heart failure. You were also given medication to help regulate the rate of your heart. The excess fluid was successfully removed from your body and your heart rate was better controlled. You were able to be discharged from the hospital and return to your assisted living facility.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: The patient was an ___ year old active male with a history of recently diagnosed atrial fibrillation, who presented with intermittent exertional chest pressure and shortness of breath. A trans thoracic echocardiogram showed depressed EF of 25% and global left ventricular hypokinesis. . # CORONARIES: Given his somewhat recently discovered partial left bundle branch block, symptoms concerning for exertional angina and heart failure, and global hypokinesis with an EF of 25% on TTE, ischemic heart disease was initially suspected. However, cariac catheterization revealed no angiographically apparent flow limiting coronary artery disease. Other causes of global left ventricular hypokinesis such as infectious cardiomyopathy, infiltrative cardiomyopathy (hemochromatosis versus amyloid disease), inflammatory cardiomyopathy (sarcoidosis), and toxic (alcoholic) cardiomyopathy were considered. Outpatient cardiology followup was arranged for further diagnostic workup. -continued baby aspirin daily . # PUMP/systolic CHF exacerbation: His TTE in ___ had been grossly normal, but his EF on this admission was depressed at 25% with global LV hypokinesis. The cause of his cardiomyopathy was not ischemic based on his cardiac catheterization. Please see the discussion above. Another cause of his dyspnea on exertion could be a primary pulmonary process and idiopathic pulmonary hypertension. The patient was diuresed with intravenous furosemide with good effect and resolution of his shortness of breath. . # RHYTHM: Atrial fibrillation, diagnosed just two weeks ago, which has been rate controlled on metoprolol. Differntial includes hyperthyroidism, ischemic or other cardiomyopathy, and alcohol use. His CHADS2 score was 2 (age and CHF). He was started on coumadin, becoming therapeutic with an INR of 2.7 on ___. His INR was 2.0 upon discharge. -Continued coumadin with a heparin bridge for his cardiac catheterization -followup TSH as an outpatient . # Acute Kidney Injury: Resolved. Creatinine went from baseline of 1 to 1.4 to 1.2 on ___. This could represent pre-renal azotemia with poor forward flow. The patient also recieved intravenous lasix, which could also have been the cause of an elevated creatinine. . # history of Depression -Continue nortryptiline . FEN: Cardiac Diet, salt restriction, fluid restriction 1.5L/day . PROPHYLAXIS: -DVT ppx with coumadin -Pain management with tylenol prn -Bowel regimen with colace/senna . CODE: Full Code . COMM: Patient
summary: You came to the hospital because you were having shortness of breath. We found that you had a condition called congestive heart failure with fluid in your lungs, and treated you with invravenous lasix. This resulted in a significant decrease in the amount of fluid in your lungs. In addition, an echocardiogram or ultrasound of your heart showed that the pumping function of your heart was significantly diminished compared to your prior test from ___. Your cardiac catheterization showed that your coronary arteries were normal. We have set you up with an outpatient cardiologist for further work-up of your heart disease.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M PMHx CVAs with residual focal neurologic deficits, CAD, chronic systolic CHF, peripheral vascular disease, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation on warfarin admitted ___ with septic shock secondary to acute cholecystitis with impacted gallstone in the cystic duct, subsequently status post percutaneous cholecystostomy placement, on antibiotics, recent course complicated by worsening leukocytosis of unclear etiology # Septic Shock secondary to Acute Cholecystitis Patient was admitted to the ICU with leukocytosis, fever, with exam and imaging concerning for acute cholecystitis. His course was complicated by worsening leukocytosis. Infectious workup did not yield any additional potential sources for infection. Per discussion with family regading risks of aspiration, they were comfortable accepting risk of potential aspiration, as long as patient was able to eat the foods he wanted to eat. # Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation Continued Warfarin # COPD: Continued home Advair, albuterol # Prior CVA: continued aspirin, statin
summary: You were admitted with a serious infection of your gallbladder (acute cholecystitis). You were treated with antibiotics and a drain in your gallbladder. During your hospital stay you were also found to have difficulty with swallowing. You and your family decided that it was more important to be able to eat the foods you like, then to not be able to eat these foods, even if there was a risk for aspirating these foods. The Visiting Nurse needs to draw an INR, CBC and Liver Function Tests on ___ (this ___. Your INR on discharge was 1.7, we are increasing your dose to 2mg daily from 1.5. summary: You were admitted to ___ after a fall. You fractured your right hip and had Orthopedic Surgery to repair your hip. To prevent development of a clot, you were started on Lovenox. You also developed some difficulty breathing after surgery and needed oxygen to help; please continue to work with Physical Therapy and use your incentive spirometer to keep your lungs open.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo morbidly obese female with history of fibromyalgia, osteoarthritis, HTN, and DM presented to the ED with hypoxemia, likely a combination of underlying hypoventilation likely secondary to obesity and narcotics with a component of diastolic heart failure. ICU Course: Admitted for hypoxia. ABG consistent with chronic hypoventilation. CTA negative for PE, but with evidence of pulmonary edema. Working diagnosis was pulmonary edema (hypoxia, peripheral edema, orthopnea) in setting of chronic hypoventilation of obesity. She was diuresed with IV Furosemide and negative 4L in 24 hours. Oxygen saturation improved to 92-94% on ___ by NC. No antibiotics were given on arrival to ICU as felt likely to not have pneumonia. Echo was done at bedside that showed....... Additionally, she was noted to be on multiple sedating medications for chronic pain/depression. Doses were confirmed with pharmacy. Her large doses of sedating meds at night likely contributing to chronic retention. Medical floor course: # Hypoxemia: Likely combination of decompensated heart failure, and hypoventilation from narcotics and obesity. Diuresed well to lasix, with improvement in SOB and hypoxemia. She will benefit from an outpatient sleep study. # Diastolic heart failure: Signs and symptoms of acute on potentially undiagnosed chronic dHF with an mildly elevated BNP which is often underestimated in the setting of obesity. She was diuresed with lasix boluses. Her beta blocker was continued, and an ACE inhibitor was initiated. # Tachycardia: Stable for patient given prior office notes. CTA negative for PE. Most likely a result of chronic pain. Improved with diuresis. # Fibromyalgia/Chronic pain: She was continued on her home dose of Cymbalta, Oxycontin, and Oxycodone. # Hypertension: Normotensive during admission. A clonidine taper was initiated while in house and will be continued as an outpatient. She was started on an ACE inhibitor which was uptitrated as the clonidine was decreased. # Diabetes: A1C 6.4 in ___. Held Metformin while inpatient and in setting of recent CTA. Sugars well controlled, did not required insulin coverage. # Depression: Mood stable and appropriate. Continued on home duloxetine, trazodone, diazepam, and keppra.
summary: You were admitted for low oxygen levels. This is most likely a result of lower than normal respiratory rates, which are likely a result of being overweight, taking large doses of narcotics, and possibly sleep apnea, as we discussed. However, a sleep study would be required to confirm this, and you should discuss consultation with a pulmonary (lung) doctor with your primary care doctor. In addition, you have a component of diastolic heart failure where your heart is stiff and does not pump as effectively. This results in fluid accumulation. You were given diuretics to help remove some of this fluid.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year-old woman with Afib, CHF, depression and dementia who presented from ___ with complaint of recurrent fevers without clear etiology. # Fevers- Per report patient was febrile to 101 at ___ ___, although patient remained afebrile in the ED and throughout her admission (Tmax 100.2). WBC count throughout admission ranged between 11 and 14K. Patient had no localizing symptoms for infection; infectious workup was negative (UA negative, CT Torso unremarkable other than thyroid nodule and ovarian cyst, TTE without endocarditis). Drug fever or malignancy were also considered, although again no obvious culprit or localizing findings and no objective fever identified during admission. CT torso and SPEP negative for malignancy. CA125 was 75, but this is non-specific. Transvaginal ultrasound also showed simple ovarian cyst. Would follow with annual ultrasound. Speech and swallow study performed to eval silent aspiration as a potential source of fever; patient did well and nutrition recommended thin liquids and soft solids. ESR, CRP and RF were quite elevated, prompting rheumatology consult. Elevated RF is unspecific. Hepatology serologies were negative. ___ and ANCA were negative, making vasculitis less likely. Physical exam not c/w inflammatory arthritis. GCA was considered as a cause of FUO in the elderly. However, she has good temporal pulses, no TTP over scalp/temple area on exam and no HAs, no vision changes. As she remained afebrile, she is discharged back to ___ ___ without clear explanation for her prior fevers. # Acute CHF exacerbation - Patient was noted to have desaturation at ___ that improved rapidly to 99% on 2L NC. Chest X-ray on admission was consistent with pulmonary edema and patient had crackles on exam, likely caused by her known CHF. Patient received 20 mg IV lasix with considerable improvement to 95% on RA on the day after admission. She is discharge on her home lasix dose. # Ovarian cyst: 1.9 cm simple cyst found on CT torso. CA125 was 75, but this is non-specific. Transvaginal ultrasound also showed simple ovarian cyst. Would follow with annual ultrasound. # Thyroid nodules: 8mm thyroid nodule seen on CT torso. Please f/u as outpatient. # Afib - Patient was continued on her home digoxin (dig level 1.3), warfarin and metoprolol. INR consistently low on home dose 2mg daily, so uptitrated warfarin to 2.5mg daily. INR on the day of discharge was 1.8. Please continue to titrate to INR goal ___ at rehab. # HLD - Patient was continued on lipitor. # Depression - Patient was continued on cymbalta. Transitional Issues: - code status: full code - new medications: none - follow up: at ___ - pending studies: Blood cultures from ___ no growth at the time of discharge - follow up issues: 1. INR 1.8 on discharge, coumadin was increased from 2mg to 2.5mg daily. Warfarin dose titration should be continued following discharge at ___. 2. thyroid nodule 3. ovarian cyst 4. nutrition: recommended thin liquid, soft solids, and 1:1 supervision
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for a temperature to 100.1 and shortness of breath at ___. You were evaluated and treated by the medicine service. You were found to have no evidence of a urinary tract infection or pneumonia. You did have some fluid in your lungs (pulmonary edema) that was treated with diuretics. Your blood tests were reassuring for the absence of an infection and you did not have a fever during the time you were in the hospital. On CT scan, we found a simple ovarian cyst that will need annual follow up. You also have a small 8mm thyroid nodule that can be followed up as outpatient. You had a speech and swallow study here, which showed that you had good swallowing abilities.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ female with a past medical history significant for CAD s/p CABG and ICD placement, vascular dementia, and HTN issues who presents after an unwitnessed fall at her nursing home. Per the patient's report, this was a mechanical fall and she reports no LOC, but is a poor historian due to dementia. She was found to have a urinary tract infection and new systolic CHF. . ACTIVE ISSUES: # FALL: She had negative cardiac biomarkers and no changes on EKG making cardiac ischemia unlikely. There were no events on telemetry, making arrhythmia an unlikely cause; likewise, interogation of her pacer revealed no events. She was not orthostatic. She was found to have a UTI which may have triggered the fall, this was treated as below. . # SPINE INJURY: On admission she had mild tenderness to palpation of the cervical spine and significant tenderness to palpation of the thoracic and lumbar spine. CT scan performed in the ED revealed old compression fractures in the thoracic and lumbar spine. Flexion-extension films of the c-spine were performed on ___ and were determined to be negative for displacement or acute injury requiring bracing. Ortho-spine was consulted in the ED and determined that the fractures were likely not acute and did not require bracing or operative management. The patient was placed in a soft collar at night for comfort. She complained of significant pain in her lower back on admission and was treated with tylenol and tramadol ___ mg Q6 PRN, with control of the pain. . # URINARY TRACT INFECTION: Urine culture grew e coli resistant to cipro and ampicillin. She was started on Bactrim double strength BID on ___ for a 5 day course to end on ___. She was febrile on ___ and afebrile thereafter. # Congestive Heart Failure: Patient was found to have mild hypoxia with pulmonary edema on exam and chest x ray. Negative cardiac enzymes. TTE showed mild CHF consistent with known CAD (regional AK, ___) and EF of 45-50% implying acute systolic and diastolic heart failure. She was continued on carvedilol and lisinopril. Lasix was added. She will follow up with cardiology as an outpatient. She will need a labs check later this week to ensure electrolyte stability on lasix. . Transitional issues: 1. Complete Bactrim course for UTI 2. Has new diagnosis of heart failure. Will require follow up with a cardiologist. Labs have been requested for ___ to ensure electrolytes stable on lasix; lasix titration PRN.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital following a fall ar your nursing home in which you hit your head. There was some question regarding whether or not you lost consciouness before the fall, so you were admitted for evaluatino to be be sure you were not at risk for episodes of losing consciousness. During your time as an inpatient, a full work up for syncopal (passing out) episodes was performed and it was determined that there was no obvious risk for you to have these episodes. Therefore, the fall was most likely not the result of a loss of consciousness. In addition to the above, you were treated for your pain from the fall and from past spine disease.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yoF with PMH of CAD, COPD, HTN, HLD, hepatitis C who presented with worsening dyspnea and RUQ pain, found to have CHF exacerbation and cholangitis. # Cholangitis: She had RUQ ultrasound showing gall stones and GB sludge, along with elevated LFTs, fever, and gram negative rod bacteremia. She had an ERCP on ___ with a biliary stent placed. Her LFTs trended down thereafter and her RUQ pain improved. She was put on zosyn for empiric gram negative coverage. By discharge, her cultures were back with enterobacter aerogenes and antibiotics were narrowed to ciprofloxacin with plans for 14 day course (last day ___. She has a follow up appointment scheduled with ERCP and will need her stent removed in ___ weeks. She also is scheduled with general surgery for consideration of cholecystectomy. # Acute on chronic systolic CHF: #Demand related MI Pt with fluid overload on exam and CXR, and proBNP of 3945. She also came in hypertensive to the 160s. She was put on a nitro drip and IV lasix. Her troponins were positive (peak of value of 0.4 on ___ with flat CKMB and no EKG changes, suggesting a form of demand ischemia in setting of sepsis. Of note, she had a repeat echo with worse systolic function than her echo in ___ (EF of 45-50% with new septal hypokinesis). Subjective symptoms improved with diuresis. She was diuresed with lasix 60mg IV BID initially, and then 60mg IV daily. She was then transitioned back to her home dose of lasix of 60mg PO daily. Her weight on discharge was 102kg. She continued both metoprolol and her lisinopril dose will be increased to 20mg on discharge. She is on ASA and statin as well. #COPD: presentation not consistent with COPD exacerbation. No wheezing on exam. PFTs and CXR not consistent with COPD. Continued prn nebs and inhalers. #Depression: Continued her current meds. She does not recall use of effexor, but given this is included on prior medication lists, it will be continued on discharge home. #Alcohol abuse: Was put on a CIWA although not clear if she had any alcohol use prior to admission. Also given MVI, thiamine and folate. Her initial plan was to return to ___, but after patient expressed strong desire to return home, she was re-evaluated by ___ and was cleared to return home with home ___ and home ___. She reported that she had sufficient 02 supply at home with several full tanks and also her concentrator. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Needs outpatient follow up with ERCP and Surgery; Her stent will need to be removed in ___ weeks - Daily weights and titration of lasix as outpatient - Cardiology Followup - Complete antibiotic course DISCHARGE EXAM: weight 102kg 97.7 146/50 to 118/61 HR ___ regular, 18, 100% RA JVP obscurred, not well visualized breath sounds clear bilaterally L ankle with kerlex dresing no peripheral edema
summary: You were admitted for pain in your abdomen and difficulty breathing. You had a procedure done called ERCP which showed that you had gallstones. A stent was placed in your bile duct to help the gallstones drain out of the gall bladder. You should have another ERCP in 4 weeks to remove the stent. You also should see a surgeon in the next several months to discuss removal of your gall bladder. In regards to your breathing, we feel that your heart was not pumping properly. This likely caused a back up of fluid from the heart to the lungs, which made it hard to breathe. You were given medications to help get rid of some of the fluid and to help your heart work less hard.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ female with a h/o dCHF (LVEF > 55%, TTE in ___, CAD s/p RCA stent, third degree heart block s/p DDDR PPM, IDDM, CKD, and HTN who p/w 3 days of SOB, found to be in acute decompensated diastolic CHF. # Acute decompensated diastolic CHF: Patient initially presented with acute dyspnea, PND, and orthopnea, and was found to be volume overloaded on exam with an elevated BNP 7874 (up from 4987 in ___ and pulmonary edema on CXR. Last documented LVEF>55% on TTE in ___, patient will ___ as an outpatient for TTE to document current EF. No acute ischemic changes on EKG, though notably in the setting of dependent AV pacing on EKG, there are ___ wave changes that makes ischemic changes difficult to interpret. Troponins were elevated, but since worsening renal function confounded interpretation, ___ was obtained and was found to be reassuring. Symptomatically improved with supplemental oxygen and lasix diuresis with 40 mg IV, which was subsequently transitioned to 40 mg po and on d/c. Physical therapy evaluated her, confirming functional mobility at baseline with ambulatory saturations > 90%, and recommended discharge home or home with ___, though patient refused home ___ services. Cardiac rehabilitation was also suggested. # CAP: Could not r/o pneumonia. Treated empirically initially with ceftriaxone and azithromycin though subsequently transitioned to levofloxacin (renally dosed to 250 mg QD) on ___ for completion on ___. Respiratory symptoms improved to near baseline prior to discharge. # CAD s/p RCA stent: Troponins remained at 0.02 during admission in the setting of upward trending Cr, though no new changes to baseline prior EKG were observed. She was continued on isosorbide mononitrate (Extended Release) 30 mg, ASA 325 mg daily, and BB (atenolol switched to metoprolol given her renal dysfunction). # IDDM c/b peripheral neuropathy: Followed by ___. Newly started linagliptin 5 mg QD. Hypoglycemic in ED in setting of poor PO intake. She was initially treated with her home dose of glargine 12 Units QHS, though this was lowered to 6 Units given AM hypoglycemia on two occasions. ISS used as inpatient and other home oral hypoglycemic agents held during admission. Increased glargine to 8 units QHS on d/c. # AoCKD: Upward trending Cr from 2.9 to 3.0 during admission, likely ___ in the setting of diuresis. Baseline Cr 2.6 at recent discharge on ___, though in general renal function has been worsening over the past year, with Cr upwards trending since ___. summary: You were admitted to ___ for shortness of breath. We performed lab tests and obtained an ___ of your chest, which showed fluid in your lungs, called pulmonary edema, and believe your shortness of breath is caused by heart failure. We started you on a medication, called lasix, which helped to decrease the fluid in your lungs, and your breathing improved. We would like you to continue the lasix at 40 mg, once a day, after you leave the hospital. Your chest ___ also showed some findings that suggested you may also have had a pneumonia. We started you on an antibiotic, called levofloxacin, and your symptoms improved. While in the hospital, your blood sugars were low, so we decreased your home insulin (glargine) dose. Your blood sugars stabilized on the lower dose of insulin. Lastly your kidney function was abnormal.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with multiple myeloma, spinal stenosis, DM2, CKD, CHF, depression who presents with falls. # Encephalopathy: He presented to the floor with toxic-metabolic encephalopathy. Likely secondary to administration of antipsychotics (IM zyprexa). The patient becomes significantly delirious with this medication. He improved after holding these medications. No evidence of infection or other cause of encephalopathy was found. Imaging of his head was without acute pathology. Of note, he has a somewhat difficult personality and is at times "feisty" and extremely sarcastic however was deemed to have capacity by PCP and HCP and on day of discharge was clear, competent and articulate. Intelligent and fully competent of making medical decisions. His HCP noted he was at his baseline mental status prior to discharge. At times he would have sundowning with some delirium overnight. # Frequent falls: He has chronic falls and has been evaluated multiple times for falls. After discussing with HCP, it seems many of his falls are related to not using his walker at home. He worked with ___ and they recommended rehab for strengthening and balance training. In addition, he will need OT training. # Acute renal failure: Secondary to urinary retention # Urinary retention: Thought to be related to antipsychotics. He required foley placement with improvement in creatinine. He had a voiding trial and had normal urine output which continued during admission with stable Cr after foley removed. # Anemia: He has normocytic anemia with baseline Hct around ___ which is chronic in nature and remained at baseline during admission. No evidence of acute bleed. # Acute on chronic diastolic heart failure: LVEF >55%. He was significantly fluid overloaded on admission. He was treated with lasix and metolazone. With this his lower extremity edema improved and he was discharged at 157lbs, near his dry weight of 155. Continued home diuretics on discharge # Hypokalemia: He required potassium supplementation when he takes his lasix/metolazone. He was discharged on home potassium supplementation. # Constipation: He was treated with a bowel regimen. # DMII: He reports good control with "diet and exercise". He FSBS were adequately controlled without significant insulin requirement. Transitional issues: - Can be difficult at times but is redirectable, seems more personality related than agitated delirium - Monitor UOP, if dropping may be retaining again and may require foley replacement - Full Code - HCP ___ (friend) ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after a number of falls at home. In the ED you were given a medication due to agitation. This resulted in you becoming sleepy and confused. You slowly improved. You were with physical therapy and they recommended rehab to improve your strength and balance prior to returning home. You were agreeable for a temporary stay at rehab. While you were in the hospital you developed urinary retention and required foley catheter placement. This was discontinued and you urinated without difficulty. You also had kidney failure which improved after the foley was placed and remained stable after it was removed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: #. CAD: - The patient was admitted with chest pain that was initially controlled with nitrates and morphine and was absent by the time he arrived on the floor. His chest pain did not recur during this admission. He was ruled out for acute MI by 3 negative sets of cardiac biomarkers and his EKG was unchanged. Since this ICD firing in the setting of missed doses of lasix has been recurrent, and that his chest pain occured post ICD firing, and he is active at baseline without chest pain, it was decided that additional ischemia evaluation was not warrented. EP evaluated the patient and noted that his ICD discharge was appropriate. He will follow up with his cardiologist, Dr. ___. He was continued on his home regimen of Metoprolol, ASA/Plavix, Imdur, and Lisinopril with good effect. . #. Pump: The patient has a known ischemic cardiomyopathy with EF of 35% on a recent TTE. Acute on chronic systolic congestive heart failure. He has had a history of non-compliance to lasix. He had a significant increase in his weight. summary: You were admitted with chest pain which resolved with some nitroglycerin. Laboratory tests showed no damage to your heart and no signs of a heart attack. You were also given some IV Lasix to assist with the removal of fluid. You also were found to have an area of redness on your abdomen. This may be a small localized infection from the heparin shots. We will give you an antibiotic to clear this up. We have also altered your procainamide dosing as your current form has been discontinued by the manufacturer. It will now be Procainamide SR, 1500mg (2 750mg tabs) 4 times a day. This is available at the ___, ___. in ___, and will be availabe around 11am on ___. We will also give you 2 doses to take today, one around 6pm and one at bedtime. We have also made an appointment for you with Dr. ___ in the ___ clinic for further CHF management.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ gentleman with ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF 70%), CAD s/p DES, AFib on Apixiban, DMII, and schizophrenia, who presented with hypoxia at pain clinic and was found to be fluid overloaded with severe dCHF exacerbation. # CORONARIES: Known 2 vessel CAD, cath on ___ showed clean coronaries # PUMP: Hx of ischemic cardiomyopathy, most recent ECHO w/ preserved LVEF of 70%, RV dilation and severe pulmonary hypertension # RHYTHM: Afib @ 80 # Acute on Chronic Diastolic Heart Failure Hx of ischemic cardiomyopathy, however, most recent ECHO showed preserved ejection fraction with severe pulmonary hypertension and elevated RV pressures. Patient presented with fluid overload on exam. RHC on ___ showed elevated wedge pressure which is consistent with volume overload and increased LVEDP which is consistent with dCHF as cause of fluid overload and severe pulmonary hypertension, although there is most likely a component of pulmonary hypertension that is not cardiac related. Pt was admitted to the ICU after cardiac catheterization for hypercarbic respiratory failure, thought to be secondary to increased dose of risperidone. The patient was intubated. He was started on propofol for sedation. pCO2 was monitored and gradually down trending on mechanical ventilation. The patient's propofol was weaned, and his mental status improved. He self-extubated that evening. The patient was diuresed with Lasix gtt for optimization of hemodynamics. He was diuresed on a Lasix gtt with metolazone. He was then transitioned to furosemide 160 qAM and 80 mg qPM upon discharge with metolazone 5 mg BID. summary: You were admitted to ___ for low oxygen levels after your procedure in pain clinic. You were found to have severe diastolic heart failure and right heart failure. A cardiac catheterization was done which showed that you had elevated pressures in your lung and heart and that you had fluid overload in your body and lungs. After the procedure you had low oxygen in your blood and you were not breathing well, so you were intubated and sent to the ICU. You were given IV Lasix to help remove extra fluid in your body. You were extubated and breathing well on your own and sent to the floor. You were continued on high doses of Lasix to remove more fluid. You will be discharged on increased doses of Lasix and new medication metolazone.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with diastolic cardiomyopathy, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation on amiodarone and Coumadin who is admitted with evidence of decompensated diastolic heart failure in the setting of diarrhea, found to have unresolved urinary tract infection (s/p Bactrim) and mild c diff colitis who was briefly diuresed to euvolemic and discharged stably with close follow up with PCP, cardiology and home services. # Acute on Chronic Diastolic heart failure (EF >= 55%): Ms. ___ presented from home following subacute onset of dyspnea. Stated that she went to visit her boyfriend at his ALF 9 days before admission which had Noro outbreak. Subsequently developed diarrhea and nausea which resolved. However, also endorsed dysuria and was treated with short course of Bactrim. In the interim, for about the week PTA, she endorsed been feeling short of breath and unsteady on her feet with associated dyspnea. Then presented to ___ where found to be in acute on chronic diastolic HF and. Some nonspecific abdominal bloating on ROS. Infectious work up revealed very dirty UA and in setting of bloating and recent abx for prior (?unresolved) UTI, a positive C diff. She was gently diuresed with iv Lasix x48 hours and treated for multiple infectious etiologies. She was remarkably stable and functional while admitted. Given 40mg PO Lasix on day of discharge and discharged with 20mg Lasix PRN for weight gain and close follow up. # C diff colitis As above, likely developed in setting of norovirus altering gut flora and recent UTI treatment with Bactrim leading to superimposed C diff colitis. Afebile w/o leukocytosis. No abdominal tenderness and only 2 BM in last 24 hours, thus no ileus. Initiated on PO flagyl for mild c diff colitis ___. -Will complete 14 day course Flagyl 500mg PO q8h for mild c diff colitis (d1: ___ end ___ # Urinary tract infection: Evidenced by grossly positive UTI, despite treatment earlier this month with Bactrim. Macrobid started in ED. F/u urine culture negative. At no point did pt have complaints of dysuria. Completed 4 day course of Nitrofurantoin 100mg PO q12h (___). ================ CHRONIC ISSUES: ================ # Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation: CHADS2 score is 4, on Coumadin with therapeutic INR. Was well within range on admission and very stable. TSH checked in setting of CHF exacerbation and amioderone use and was wnl. - continued Amiodarone 200 mg PO/NG DAILY; - continued Warfarin 4 mg PO/NG DAILY # Hypertension: BP mildly elevated on admission to 160s systolic. Did not want to overcorrect and used threshold of SBP <150. Increased amlodipine to 10mg daily, added Carvedilol 6.25mg twice daily and continued home losartan at 160mg daily. # Aortic stenosis: Last TTE ___: Mild aortic valve stenosis (valve area 1.2-1.9cm2). Trace aortic regurgitation is seen. Mild to moderate (___) mitral regurgitation is seen. It was felt that her AS was not contributing to her current exacerbation given infectious etiologies and response to relatively gentle iv diuresis. CONTINUED: # Diet controlled diabetes: Diabetic diet. # HLD: Atorvastatin 10 mg PO/NG QPM # GERD: Continue Omeprazole 20 mg PO BID # Depression: Continued Sertraline 37.5 mg PO/NG DAILY # Chronic pain: Continued gabapentin and lidocaine patch *****TRANSITION ISSUES***** # CONTACT: daughter, ___, ___ # CODE STATUS: full, confirmed # Follow up volume status and weight (discharged on PRN Lasix) # Follow up BP; increased amlodipine FROM 7.5mg TO 10mg daily and added Carvedilol 6.25mg twice daily # c.diff treatment for 14-day course(course of Flagyl 500mg PO q8h ___ # f/u current UTI treated with macrobid # Bactrim may have predisposed to C diff colitis and UTI on admission responsive to Nitrofurantoin. Would recommend avoiding Bactrim and Abx w/anaerobic activity in future if possible. # No changes to Coumadin, routine follow up at PCP ___ # Discharge Weight: 67.8kg
summary: You were admitted to the ___ for evaluation of your shortness of breath. You were determined to have a mild exacerbation of your (diastolic) heart failure. You were given intravenous and pill forms of a medication called "Lasix" to help you remove this excess fluid that had built up causing your difficulty breathing. You responded very well to these medications. Additionally, we noted you were having some loose bowel movements and your abdominal exam was slightly abnormal. We sent a stool test for a bacteria called Clostridium difficile (C. diff) which returned positive. As we discussed with your daughters present, this is a bacteria that many individuals have in their intestines normally. You did not do anything or eat anything which caused it to be there. It is likely that you developed an infection related to this bacteria in the setting of the diarrheal illness you had a week or two ago and the antibiotics you were given for a urinary tract infection called "Bactrim" (sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim). While this medication is NOT an allergy, you should be cautious in taking it in the future as it is not uncommonly indicated in predisposing individuals to this infection. Finally, your urinary tract infection you previously had had not quite resolved. You were given a short course of medication "Nitrofurantoin" or "MacroBid.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ F with multiple medical problems including hx of CAD s/p CABG x ___ complicated by wound infection, AICD placement ___ after VT, C. diff who presents today with tachyarrhythmia and AICD firing. #. AICD firing/tachycardia: ECG most consistent with multifocal atrial tachycardia. There was no clear precipitant. It was possibly due to wound infection and irrigation of the myocardium vs. myocarditis due to extension of infection. It also may have been exacerbated by CHF, as patient appears to be volume overloaded with high ProBNP of 10306. ___ be ischemic in origin, though enzymes were negative. Given history of PE, this is also a possible precipitant for tachycardia, since this patient was also not anticoagulated. However, ___ u/s were negative for DVT. Her HR was well controlled on metoprolol 50mg BID. The EP serivce performed AICD interogation this which showed 7 episodes of shock at rates above 188. Adjustments were made by the EP service. She had no further episodes of MAT prior to discharge. #. Acute sytolic CHF: ECHO done after admission showed EF of 25%, which is down from normal EF 3 months ago. She was markedly volume overloaded on admission with significant peripheral edema. She was started on a furosemide gtt with over 6L of fluid removed. She was restarted on her PO furosemide prior to discharge. Unclear cause of acute heart failure though there doesn't seem to have been a new ischemic event based on ECG or enzymes. Lisinopril was restarted prior to discharge. She will be continued on aspirin, statin, and metoprolol. She will followup with Dr. ___ in ___, at which time she will need a repeat echocardiogram to assess for any improvement of her EF. She should have daily weights, and a weekly creatinine to evaluate renal function while on furosemide #. Sternotomy wound: Pt was discontinued on her previous antibiotics regimen of Vancomycin and Meropenam due to new sensitivity results. ___ sets of blood culutres on admission grew VRE (___). Repeat blood cultures remained negative. ID recommended changing to ampicillin 2gm IV Q4H and PICC removal, cath tip cx negative. New midline PICC was placed (could not go past midline) and per IV team, this would be adequate for 2 weeks of IV ampicillin for total of 2 weeks (last day ___. She remained afebrile and blood cx negative. They also recommended that metronidazole be continued for 7 days after discontinuation of IV antibiotics (last day ___. A repeat wound culture grew Psuedomonas Aeruginosa which is only sensitive to Amikacin and resistant to Meropenem, but ID felt this was likely a colonizer since she did not have any evidence of acute infection. Plastics evaluated the wound, and recommended placing a wound vac, and the recommendations are per them. Wound care: Site: sternal wound Type: Surgical Dressing: VAC, Continuous, Black Foam, Target Presure 125 mm Hg Comment: to bedside, Plastic Surgery will place and change q3d #. Hx of C. Diff: Patient had no significant diarrhea while she was admitted. She will continue Flagyl 500mg PO TID (last day ___ #. Hx of PE: Pt was anticoagulated for 2 months following her PE in ___ and had multiple bleeding episodes. Following hem/onc consult at the time, they placed the pt on prophylactic dosage of Lovenox. She was maintained on prophylaxis here. She had Ultrasound of upper and lower extremities which were negative for DVTs. #. Meningioma: continued Dexamethasone at current dose 1mg PO BID. #. Psych: continued Citalopram, Ativan, Abilify #. ACCESS: R PICC line #. Comm: ___ ___. Pt states her son is her healthcare proxy. #. CODE STATUS: FULL **She should have daily weights, K checked every other day, and a weekly creatinine to evaluate renal function while on furosemide
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for a very fast heart rate and your ICD firing multiple times. You were found to have an irregular fast rhythm called Multifocal atrial tachycardia. The EP cardiologists adjusted the settings on your device. You also had low blood pressures initially, which were treated with IV fluids. You had significant extra fluid from new heart failure, with an ejection fracture of 25%. We gave you furosemide IV to take fluid off, and you had over 6 liters of fluid removed with marked improvement in the swelling of your legs. You had a blood culture which grew enteroccous, and the wound culture grew pseudomonas, but the infectious disease doctors ___ that this was likely a colonizer. You will be treated with ampicillin until ___. You will also need to continue the metronidazole until ___. The plastic surgeons saw your wound, and a wound vac was placed. This will be continued to be followed by the wound care people per the plastic surgery recommendations.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo man with chronic cough, native vessel coronary artery disease and diastolic heart failure not exacerbated, benign hypertension and hyperlipidemia presented with 2 days of acute exacerbation of chronic cough of nine weeks. 1. Chronic cough: Given worseing over past week and low-grade fever in ED could be exacerbated by bronchitis but given chronic nature, additional contributing factors were suspected. He was treated with levofloxacin for any possible contribution of acute bronchitis. He denied symptoms of rhinitis (making post-nasal drip unlikely) but did acknowledge some symptoms of GERD. Therefore he was started empirically on a PPI. Underlying COPD was considered likely given his 50 pack-year smoking history and occupational inhalation exposrue and PFT's were performed showing an FEV1/FVC ration < 70%, and an FEV1 of 60% predicted improving to 66% predicted post-albuterol. His response to duoneb inhaler suggested a reactive component. CHF was considered as a possible cause of his cough. He did seem to have some transient relief with diuretics in the past, but TTE showd normal ejection fraction and he did not appear volume overloaded on exam. Given his tobacco history and occupational exposures, a CT chest was done to further characterize paraenchyma/pleural thickening. It showed diffuse centrilobular emphysema, dependent opacities in the trachea, R main ___ bronchus, and broncus intermedius likely representing secretions, and several findings that should be followed-up with a repeat chest CT in three months, including a new 7 mm RLL nodule, septal thickening and nodularity of RML, and enlargement of R retrocrural lymph node. Blood cultures were drawn and no growth to date during his hospitalization, and should be followed-up after discharge. A CT chest to re-exam the pulmonary nodule and RML septal thickening is recommended by radiology in three months. If the abnormalities presist pulmonary consultation may be warrented. Both Mr. ___ translator) and his PCP ___ informed of these findings at the time of their discovery. 2. GERD: Suspected clinically based on symptoms and may be contributing to chronic cough, therefore he ws started on empiric 1 month trial of PPI therapy. If this does not seem to help it should be discontinued. 3. Pulmonary nodule: As described above noted on CT chest. Follow up CT warrented in 3 months 4. COPD: PFTs showed Gold Stage II moderate COPD pattern. Final read of PFT's pending at time of discharge. He was therefore started daily tiotropium and albuterol as needed at the time of discharge. 5. Chronic diastolic heart failure: A TTE was performed which showed preserved EF (70%), mild LVH, mild MR, and increased LV filling pressures. The patient appeared euvolemic. His home regimen of lasix was continued. ACE inhibitor may be considered in the future in addition to beta-blocker and diuretic. 6. CAD, native vessel: No chest pain. He was continued on beta-blocker, statin, aspirin. 7. Hyperlipidemia: His statin was continued. 8. Hypertension, benign: He was normotensive during admission and was kept on metoprolol. 9. Insomnia: Continued on ambien 2.5mg qhs prn. Full code.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with a diagnosis of acute bronchitis and Gastrointestial reflux disease (GERD). Your chronic cough is likely partially caused by acid reflux from GERD. You were started on omeprazole for this problem. There may also be a component of obstructive lung disease as a CT of your chest showed emphysema and secretions in your airways. The CT also showed several nodules that should be followed-up with another CT in three months. The acute worsening of your symptoms were likely caused by an acute bronchitis. You were treated with levaquin (an antibiotics) for the bronchitis.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with h/o afib, sCHF (EF 35%), DM, CKD (Baseline Cr 2) who presents with ___ episodes low volume, melanotic stools x 1 day and bilateral lower abdominal pain, concerning for ischemic colitis. ACTIVE ISSUES: #Severe sepsis: Patient met ___ SIRS (leukocytosis, HR in high ___ in setting of beta-blocker use) on admission with concern for GI source of infection. On presentation she had signs of end organ hypoperfusion with ___ and a lactic acidosis. In ED had CTA showing evidence of enteritis and was given fluids, empirically started on Vanc/Cefepime/Flagyl. Given hypotension on arrival she was additionally given Hydrocortisone 100mg IV x1 in setting of chronic steroid use and concern for adrenal insufficiency. In the MICU, she was continued on broad spectrum antibiotics with Vanc/Cefepime and Flagyl for concern of intraabdominal infection and was additionally started on PO Vanc until ruled out for C.diff. She continued to receive IV fluids for resuscitation. Surgery was consulted for ongoing concern that this might be an ischemic colitis and performed serial abdominal exams while patient was in the MICU. Patient lactate normalized and her renal function improved. She was transferred to the general medicine floor on ___ where she continued Cefepime/Flagyl. #Enteritis: Concern for ischemic colitis vs infectious colitis as seen on CT. Patient presented with ___ episodes of low volume dark red bloody stools x 1 day and in this setting with a lactic acidosis, patient was admitted to the MICU for close monitoring. Abdominal exam with point tenderness bilateral lower abdomen in the setting of chronic steroid use, which can mask peritonitis concerning for ischemia. She had serial abdominal exams by the MICU team and ACS and continued to improve with treatment of her sepsis as above. She continued Cefepime/Flagyl to empirically treat an intraabdominal infection. ___ on CKD: Patient with ___ on presentation, differential included pre-renal azotemia, ATN in setting of hypotension vs. obstructive given her very low urine output on presetnation. She was given IVF as above to treat her sepsis and renal function improved to baseline. #Transaminitis - Mild transaminase elevation in setting of severe sepsis. LFTs were trended and improved. CHRONIC ISSUES: #Paroxysmal Afib: on amiodarone for rhythm and metop for rate control. Warfarin for anticoagulation. INR 3.3 on presentation, Coumadin was held in setting of possible need for surgical intervention and concern for ongoing GI bleed. Her Metoprolol was additionally held in setting of sepsis. Amiodarone was continued, and Coumadin was restarted with a therapeutic range INR (2.2) on discharge. #HFrEF (EF 35%) - Hypovolemic on presentation in setting of colitis/sepsis. Her home Torsemide and Lisinopril were held on admission. She was given cautious IVF in light of her recent CHF exacerbations. Home torsemide was restarted after concern for an volume overloaded state. Respiratory status remained at baseline. # Pericardial Effusion: Seen on CT, appears stable from prior exams. # COPD: Continued tiotropium. # Depression: Continued venlafaxine. # RUL nodule: Stable from prior, needs repeat CT chest ___ per outpatient plan. Currently scheduled. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -Needs to f/u with GI in 1 week. Will need another colonoscopy -Levofloxacin 750mg QD for three days. last dose ___
summary: You were admitted the ___ following an episode of bloody stools. You were found to have a high white blood cell count and other signs of infection. You were started on antibiotics and monitored for improvement. You quickly improved while on the antibiotics. While here you developed some difficulty breathing. A chest xray was done and revealed some fluid in the lungs. This was concerning for a possible pneumonia so you were given additional antibitoics. Your breathing quickly improved over the course of a couple days. You will be discharged with a prescription for antibiotics which should be taken until ___. Based on the CT that was done there was concern for your lower colon. It was recommended that you follow-up with a GI Physician and get ___ colonoscopy.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M with H/O hypertension, hyperlipidemia, DM and CAD s/p PCI ___ for unstable angina complicated by perforation of the mid RCA and known 80% stenosis of the LAD which was not intervened upon who presented with new onset crushing chest pain, found to have angiographic evidence suggestive of persistent mid RCA perforation and stable LAD stenosis on coronary angiography ___. # NSTEMI/CAD: S/P PCI of RCA complicated by perforation treated with a covered stent ___, presented again with chest pain, lateral ST depressions and troponin-T elevation 0.5 and 1.01 consistent with NSTEMI. Anticoagulation was held given recent RCA perforation. Admission TTE showed mild LVH with preserved LV function (EF 60-65%) and stable trivial-sized pericardial effusion. Coronary angiography ___ suggested persistent RCA perforation and stable 80% LAD stenosis. Patient was placed on bed rest and intermittently required nitro drip for control of recurrent chest pain. He underwent repeat coronary angiography on ___ which showed NO pericardial leak from the prior perforation. A DES was placed in the mid LAD. His home isosorbide mononitrate dose was increased, and the patient was discharged on home doses of ASA, Plavix, beta blocker and statin with plan for follow up with Dr. ___. # ___: Admission Cr 1.8, improved to 1.5 which appeared to be his baseline. Patient was at risk for contrast induced nephropathy from the contrast load during 2 angiograms. He was hydrated, and his Cr remained 1.4-1.5. # Acute on chronic diastolic LV CHF: Patient had known chronic LV diastolic CHF and presented with signs of volume overload on physical exam in the setting of NSTEMI and IV fluid administration. We continued his home Lasix, and his weight decreased. # Hypertension: BP remained in good range. Continued home carvedilol, amlodipine, furosemide, terazosin. # Type 2 DM: On 100 units of 70/30 BID at home plus 30 units lantus and sliding scale. He had asymptomatic FSBG of 50 ___, likely due to decreased PO intake while hospitalized, so downtitrated his insulin 70/30 to 85 units AM, 80 units ___, continued home glargine. Chronic issues: # Hyperlipidemia: Continued home rosuvastatin 40 mg. # Chronic LBP: On narcotics contract through ___. Continued home gabapentin, Vicodin. # Chronic urticaria: Continued HydrOXYzine 25 mg PO QHS. # Hypothyroidism: Continued home Levothyroxine Sodium 300 mcg PO DAILY.
summary: You were admitted to ___ for a heart attack from the area of blockage in your one artery that had not been fixed on your previous cardiac catheterization. Because you still had some leaking from the right coronary artery we did not fix the left coronary artery until ___ at which time we placed another stent into your coronary artery without any problems. Your blood sugars varied a lot while you were here and your diet was different. You should check your sugars closely while you are at home. You were also having some chest pain that was associated with eating and had no evidence of heart related so we started you on an antacid called ranitidine. Your blood pressure was also high so we increased your imdur.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with a history of dementia, HFpEF (LVEF>55% in ___, chronic venous insufficiency, atrial fibrillation on dabigatran, h/o of MVR with porcine valve, and tricuspid valve repair (___) who presents with fall and hypervolemia. # FALL/FOUND DOWN: Patient found down at assisted living home, unwitnessed fall. EKG without new ischemic changes. Etiology likely in mechanical in setting of dementia given no telemetry events and no evidence of ischemia. Patient suffered a L clavicular fracture (described below). Delirogenic meds (alprazolam, codeine) were stopped given concern for worsening confusion. # ACUTE ON CHRONIC DIASTOLIC CHF EXACERBATION (LVEF>55%): On presentation, patient had new oxygen requirement. CXR showed pulmonary edema. Home dose of Lasix was Furosemide 40 mg PO DAILY, recently decreased from 60 mg due to hypotension. Exam notable for bibasilar crackles. TTE here with preserved LVEF and no further evidence of mitral stenosis as was seen on prior TTE at ___ with well seated mitral tricuspid annulplasty ring and normal functioning mitral valve bioprosthesis. She was diuresed with IV Lasix and transitioned back to her home dose of Lasix 40 mg daily and metoprolol tartrate 12.5 mg PO BID. Discharge weight: 53.8 kg. # L DISTAL CLAVICLE FRACTURE: Pt with minimally displaced L distal clavicle fracture. She was seen by orthopedic surgery who suggested wearing a sling for 2 weeks (til ___ with follow-up in their clinic. Pain controlled with acetaminophen. # ATRIAL FIBRILLATION: Patient on home Dabigatran, rate controlled with Metoprolol and Amiodarone. Had been on warfarin (indicated given valvular afib) but had changed to dabigatran due to difficulty managing frequent monitoring of INR with warfarin. After discussion with patient, patient's daughter, and team, weighed the risk/benefits of anticoagulation given her frequent falls and decided to hold off on further anticoagulation and discharged on ASA 81 mg. She will have further discussions with her outpatient cardiologist, Dr. ___ ___ about risks/benefits of anticoagulation. CHRONIC ISSUES: # DEMENTIA: She was continued on her home dose of donepezil and memantine. At night, she did sundown and became agitated for which Seroquel was given instead of her home alprazolam. QTc was 490 and decided to hold further doses of Seroquel. # GERD -Continued home Pantoprazole 40 mg PO Q12H -Continued home Sucralfate 1 gm PO QID -Continued home Ranitidine 150 mg PO DAILY # CONSTIPATION -Continued home Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO DAILY # NUTRITION -Continued home Multivitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY -Continued home Vitamin D 1000 UNIT PO DAILY -Continued home Cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg PO DAILY # DEPRESSION: -Continued home Sertraline 100 mg PO QHS Transitional issues: - Patient had been on dabigatran for anticoagulation (had previously been on warfarin but this was discontinued because of difficulty with monitoring). Due to concern for bleeding in the setting of frequent falls, this was held and she was discharged on 81 mg aspirin pending her primary cardiologist's decision RE anticoagulation. - Patient's home alprazolam and acetaminophen with codeine were discontinued due to concerns that they increased her fall risk. She was given Seroquel in the hospital for delirium/agitation with benefit but did have increased QTc to 492 so was not discharged on this medication. This could be considered in the future, but QTc should be followed carefully. - Please follow up on free T4 and consider starting levothyroxine if indicated given elevated TSH. - Discharged on 40 mg PO furosemide - Discharge weight 53.8 kg - CODE: DNR/DNI (confirmed) - CONTACT: ___, daughter, ___ (cell)
summary: You were in the hospital because you fell at home. Due to the fall, you broke your collar bone. Orthopedic surgery saw you and recommended that you wear a sling and follow up with them in clinic in 2 weeks. You should wear the sling at all times until then. You were also found to have a mild build up of fluid due to your heart failure. We gave you some medication through your IV (an IV version of the furosemide that you take at home) to remove this fluid. After leaving the hospital, you will go to a rehabilitation facility to help maintain and build up your strength. After that you may need more help to make sure that you do not continue to fall.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PRIMARY REASON FOR ADMISSION: ================================ Ms. ___ is a ___ with a hx recent admission for GIB, dCHF (EF >55%), HOCM, and pAF (on apixaban 2.5mg BID) who presented to MICU for hypotension and anemia in the setting of a GI bleed. ACTIVE ISSUES: ================================ #GI Bleed c/b hemodynamic instability: She has a history of multiple life-threatening GI bleeds and is s/p jejunal anastomasis in ___ for bleeding mass. During this admission, she was monitored in the MICU and received 2 units of pRBCs. Given recent negative workup (during previous admission ___ she had EGD, ___ and capsule endoscopy without clear source of bleeding), she underwent Meckels scan that was negative. She underwent CT A&P given abdominal pain that was also unrevealing (beyond cholelithiasis). GI recommended tagged RBC scan but given her spontaneous improvement and hemodynamic stability this was not necessary and she was instead transferred to the floor. As this bleed occurred in the setting of a reduced dose of apixaban (halved during previous admission), her outpatient cardiologist felt the risk was too great to continue systemic anticoagulation. She will be discharged on a asa 81mg in an attempt to balance risk of future bleed with risk of stroke (see below). Further GI workup and adjustment of anticoagulation to be determined by her outpatient providers. #pAtrial Fibrillation: The patient has a history of pAF with CHADS2 of 5, including a history of thalamic stroke in ___. During her previous admission, apixaban was halved to 2.5mg BID. Given this episode of bleeding, her outpatient Cardiologist recommended discontinuation of systemic anticoagulation. Her digoxin was also discontinued. Given risk and balance discussion with the patient, she will be discharged on asa 81mg with plans for follow up with her outpatient providers. Given her HR in the ___, her atenolol was also not restarted.
