HR should be closely monitored as an outpatient and beta blocker initiated as necessary. #Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy: s/p ethanol ablation in ___. She underwent repeat TTE that showed overall normal EF, TDI E/e' >13, suggesting PCWP>18mmHg with severe resting LVOT gradient significantly higher from previous TTE in ___. She will continue to follow up with her outpatient Cardiologist. CHRONIC ISSUES: ================================ #CAD (multiple stents placed in ___ and dCHF TTE ___: (LVEF > 55%, mild mitral regurgitation): Stable. She was continued on amiodarone and digoxin discontinued per her outpatient cardiologist. Discharged on asa 81mg (see above) and her home simvastatin. #PBC: Stable. No evidence of cirrhosis (grade ___ fibrosis only), no stigmata of liver disease. Her urosodiol was continued with plans for outpatient follow up with her GI physician. #Celiac disease: Continued gluten free diet. #GERD: She was initially on a pantoprazole gtt in the MICU and then transitioned to po pantoprazole when stable on the floor. #HTN: Given HR in the ___, her atenolol was held and not restarted on discharge. sbp stable on the floor. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ================================ - Please restart beta blocker pending HR (currently in the ___ to low ___ off beta blocker) - Please monitor volume status and restart torsemide when necessary. She was euvolemic at discharge and did not require further diuresis. - Would consider switching from simvastatin to low dose atorva given increased risk of rhabdo in patient's simva and amidoarone. - She will be discharged off of apixaban per her outpatient cardiologist. Given her prior stroke, we started aspirin 81mg on discharge in attempt to balance the risk of future CVA with risk of GI bleed. This should be discussed with her outpatient providers - ___: 8.6/27.1
summary: You were admitted after having an episode of bleeding from your GI tract. You received blood products initially but fortunately the bleeding stopped on its own. Your heart rates and blood counts remained stable and you were transferred to the general medical floor. Your outpatient cardiologist recommended stopping your digoxin and apixaban given your bleeding, and you were monitored overnight without any further events. We discussed starting aspirin therapy with you and will plan to start low dose aspirin (81mg) given your history of strokes. It will be important to discuss this with your cardiologist when you see him as an outpatient.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ woman with a history of atrial fibrillation (on warfarin), CAD s/p BMS to RCA, and CKD who was admitted after a mechanical fall and found to have non-operative fractures of her pubic ramus and L1. Her course was complicated by volume overload and hypoxia, which improved with diuresis, and by labile blood sugars. ACTIVE ISSUES ------------------- 1. Likely diastolic CHF, acute on chronic: CXR in ED was consistent with pulmonary edema and BNP elevated to 3000s. She does not have systolic heart failure and most likely due to diastolic dysfunction. She was given several doses of IV lasix with improvement in her hypoxia and symptoms, eventually weaned to home lasix 80mg PO. 2. Diabetes: Initially, there was confusion about home insulin, and patinet was started on glargine 30u BID, which then switched confirmed home regimen of 70/30 65 units AM and 25 units ___. Patient became markedly hypoglycemic to the 30's at midday on ___, which may have been due to overlap of glargine and 70/30. 70/30 dose was reduced and sugars remained labile. She was discharged on 70/30 20 units AM and 15 units ___. Patient, daughter, and son-in-law were counseled that insulin requirements will likely increase on home diet; the agreed to work with ___ (who will come morning after d/c) and PCP, to slowly uptitrate insulin as needed. 3. Pubic Ramus Fracture: No indication for surgery per Orthopedics. She received pain control with acetaminophen. Activity level is weight bearing as tolerated (must wear TLSO for L1 fracture when weight bearing). 4. L1 Fracture: Fitted for a TLSO brace, which she should wear whenever weight bearing. Patient and family received teaching on brace with ___. 5. UTI: Patient had a positive UA in setting of Foley catheter. Catheter was removed and she was asymptomatic, but she developed cloudy, foul-smelling urine. Two urine cultures grew proteus. Given recent instrumentation, treatment started with Augmenting 500 mg BID x 7 days, last day ___. 5. Afib: Rate controlled with metoprolol 100mg daily. Warfarin was held in the setting of possible procedure. Instructed to resume warfarin with 6 mg on day of discharge; thereafter, will take normal daily dose of 3 mg daily. CHRONIC ISSUES 1. HTN: Continued home lisinopril and nifedipine; increased amlodipine to 10mg daily given elevated BPs. 2. CAD: Continued home metoprolol, aspirin, statin. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Take 6 mg coumadin tonight. After that, take your usual 3 mg daily. Have your INR checked at Dr. ___ office on ___ - Continue Augmentin 500 mg BID for 5 more days (last dose on the evening of ___ - Discharge insulin dose is 70/30 20 units AM and 15 units ___. Call PCP for any sugars > 400. Call PCP to talk about slow uptitration. - Spine and Ortho follow-up arranged - Wear TLSO at all times when weight-bearing - Increased amlodipine to 10 mg daily - Urine culture grew enterococcus species after initiation of treatment for Proteus UTI. Strain listed as ampicillin sensitive, but if patient develops symptoms of UTI after finishing course, would consider possibility of b-lactamase+ enterococcal strain.
summary: As you know, you were admitted after a fall. You were found to have fractures in your pelvic bone and in one of your vertebrae. Beacuse of your vertebrae fracture, you were given a brace. It is very important that you wear you brace any time you are on your feet. We have arranged follow-up appoinments with the spine doctors and with ___. During your hospitalization, your warfarin was stopped. Please take 6 mg (DOUBLE your normal dose) tonight. After that, you should go back to your normal dose of 3 mg daily. During your hospitalization, you had extra fluid in your lungs that made it hard to breath. We used medicine to remove the fluid and your breathing improved.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Pt is a ___ year old male with PMH of HTN, DM2 and vfib arrest s/p biv pacer, Ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF ___ s/p stent of AAA in ___, presented to ___ ER ___ with acute onset R flank pain. . # Flank Pain: CT abdomen at ___ was unremarkable for worsening of his AAA. He has no evidence of kidney stone given that no dysuria and U/A was clear. He has clear point tenderness over hip, which suggests that this is likely musculoskeletal in nature. Vascular surgery was consulted and they did not believe that pt had evidence of worsening dissection. Given clear CT and no lack of clinical signs, unlikely that he has intrabdominal process. Pt's pain was treated with oxycodone and flexeril. Pain improved by time of discharge. . # ___: On admission Cr was 3.4. Urine lytes from AM are consistent with prerenal etiology (Fena is 0.6). Renal US was unremarkable (with exception of b/l renal cysts) and UA clear. Could be in setting of overdiuresis. His lasix was held and maintenance IVF were started and pt's ___ improved. At time of d/c cr improved to 2.1. ___ from ___ clinic was called prior to discharge and it was agreed that his diuretics should be restarted but at a lower dose. He was discharged on lasix 40mg daily and will follow up in ___ clinic to readjust diuretics as needed. . # Gout: During hospitalization, pt's left knee demonstrated extreme point tenderness and was mildly swollen and erythematous. He has had prior gout flares in this knee before. He was started on prednisone 50mg taper and his pain improved. Uloric was held and NSAIDS/colchicine avoided because of ___. He will need to restart uloric when he follows up with PCP. Etiology of gout flare most likely ___ overdiuresis. . CHF: Pt was euvolemic on admission. After holding lasix for several days, he was restarted on lasix at 40mg daily as mentioned above. He did not have any signs of CHF exacerbation during hospitalization. He was discharged on toprol, lasix and his digoxin dose was halved. . HTN: On admission pt was normotensive. Given his ___, valsartan was held initially. He remained normotensive and given recent ___, valsartan was not restarted. This medication should be restarted as an outpt, perhaps at a lower dose. . Afib: On presentation, INR was supratherapeutic. He did not have any signs of bleed. Coumadin was held initially but restarted on HD as his INR returned to therapeutic range. At time of discharge, INR was subtherapeutic, most likely secondary to holding dose at time of admission. He will need a repeat INR check in three days time and coumadin should be adjusted to titrate INR between 2 and 3. He was continued on toprol and digoxin for rate control. . vfib hx. Pt has ICD in place. He was continued with amiodorone. . Diabetes: Pt was put on sliding scale insulin + basal lantus during hospitalization. His insulin requirements were uptitrated given hyperglycemia secondary to prednisone. At time of discharge, his BG had normalized and he was discharged on home lantus and humalog dosing. . CODE: FULL . Transitional: - needs follow up INR - follow up in ___ clinic to adjust lasix dosing as needed. - will need to restart losartan (possibly at lower dose)
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for flank pain and acute kidney injury. We worked up your flank pain with imaging, and the vascular team did not see any evidence of worsening of your aortic aneurysm. We also do not believe that you are having a kidney stone or any intraabdominal process. Your back pain is most likely musculoskeletal in nature and we encourage rest and your home pain medication regimen. During your hospitalization your were also found to have an acute kidney injury secondary to dehyrdation from your home lasix dose. After speaking with ___ we decided to restart your lasix at discharge at a dose of 40mg daily. You also had a gout flare while inpatient. We started you on prednisone, which you will take for an additional three days.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ ___ speaking (poor historian) who p/w nausea and chills and resp distress with infiltrate concern for PNA. . # PNA: on admission had new O2 req of 3L, retrocardiac opacity on CXR, concerning for community acquired PNA. WBC slightly elevated with 90% PMNs. UCx neg. sputum cannot be produced. The pt was treated with ___ renally dosed and tylenol/guaifenesin/tessylon as needed for cough. She was also given nebs prn shortness of breath. Her breathing slowly improved but her lung sounds continued to be rhonchorous. She likely also has some component of upper airway obstruction and perhaps OSA. She was discharged on ___ to be completed for a total of 10 days. Of note, the original CXR for the pt suggested that rpt imaging after resolution might be useful to r/o possible malignancy but this was not seen in rpt CXR. In the past, she has refused w/u of breast mass and other outpt findings. If pt fails to improve in future, rpt imaging or consultation with PCP ___ to talk about rpt imaging could be considered. . # Afib: Per report the pt has a h/o PAfib but on admission she was in NSR on 12.5 bid of metoprolol. During her stay she triggered three times for AF with RVR up to 140s which responded to boluses of IV dilt 10mg. She was also increased to 50 TID metoprolol and spontaneously converted back to sinus with HR in ___. Due to one of her episodes of RVR she went into flash pulmonary edema and required transfer to the ICU for diuresis and potential cardioversion (although pt converted spontaneously to sinus). She was called out of the ICU after one day when she was breathing on 2L. She is discharged to a facility for tele monitoring. She was kept on her home aspirin 325. If the pt has an episode of RVR, per her cardiologist it was recommended to not start amiodarone or dronedarone due to its high risks. She may start PO dilt on top of her metoprolol if needed but it would be most ideal to simply titrate up her metoprolol (at 37.5mg TID on dicharge). . # CAD/CHF: Pt not on lasix at home. EF 40-45. Pt did not have any CP and there was low concern for ACS although new RBBB and TWIs. She ruled out for MI with cardiac enzymes x3. As noted above, if the pt goes into RVR she is prone to flash pulmonary edema given her poor EF at baseline. She responds to IV lasix ___ mg boluses as she is relatively lasix naive. . # Gout: stable without flare this admission. We held her home colchicine 0.6 in acute setting. We treated with tylenol prn. . # CKD: pt is discharged within her baseline of 1.7-2.0 . # Social: Found in feces/urine at home. Concern for elder abuse or poor social situation. Son is in ___ and is HCP. Son is not involved in care as his wife and the pt do not get along. SW visited pt and did not find any concerns for elder abuse at this time. . # Depression: stable. we continued home celexa. . # Contact: son ___ (HCP) ___ #) Code Status: Full (confirmed with pt and son this admission) . ___ on Admission: Metoprolol tartrate 12.5 b.i.d. ECASA 325 Citalopram 20 colchicine 0.6 Voltaren gel p.r.n. fluticasone spray lactulose lidocaine patch SLTNG 0.4 p.r.n. polyethylene glycol acetaminophen vitamin C docusate ___ MVI one daily, senna 8.6 vitamin A and D ointment.
summary: You were admitted due to a pneumonia. You were initially treated with antibiotics. You suffered from some episodes of a fast heart rate called atrial fibrillation which we were able to control with medications. You were in the ICU for one day due to this leading to fluid build up in your lungs which was removed with diuretics. When you returned to the medical floor your breathing was improved and you were working with physical therapy. Your heart rate has been in control as well. Please take levofloxacin as prescribed for your pneumonia. Please take metoprolol 37.5mg three times a day for control of your heart rate. Please take guaifenesin and benzonatate as needed for cough and nebulizers as needed for shortness of breath. There were no other changes to your medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ with PMH of CAD s/p multiple PCI, sCHF (EF ___, Atrial fibrillation on rivaroxaban and hepatitis C who presents to ED with L toe pain and dyspnea in the setting of medication noncompliance (torsemide), believed to have a CHF and COPD exacerbation. He was diuresed with 60 mg IV lasix ___ times daily, and was net negative approximately 2L. Due to poor movement of air, diffuse expiratory wheezing, and cough productive of sputum, we treated him for a COPD exacerbation with a five day course of levofloxacin and prednisone (___). Podiatry evaluated the patient during this admission and felt that the patient was at baseline, without active infection, and recommended follow up in their clinic. His hospital stay was notable for syncopal event without loss of consciousness, that occured in the setting of straining for a bowel movement. CT was negative. EKG showed a. fib without RVR. Electrolytes WNL, serum tox negative, urine tox negative. It was felt to be a vagal event. He had no further events during his hospitalization. # L Foot pain: Xray in ED without evidence for acute bone destruction. Podiatry evaluated in ED and reported no signs of osteo or deeper infection appreciated. Per podiatry recommendations, no antibiotics necessary at this time. We controlled his pain with oxycodone prn. # sCHF (EF ___: Most consistent with acute exacerbation in the setting of medication noncompliance due to access to obtaining home meds. Reports some weight gain, orthopnea and dyspnea on exertion and his JVP is elevated though no ___ which he has experienced previously. pBNP elevated to 6854. We restarted torsemide 20 mg daily and continued his home carvedilol and lisinopril. We diuresed him with IV lasix for a net negative of ___ L this admission. #COPD: CXR PA/Lat in ED read as possible new opacities concerning for pneumonia. However, his CXR looks largely unchanged from prior with evidence of pulmonary edema in the setting of systolic CHF exacerbation. WBC 6 on admission. However, his WBC increased to WBC of 14. Given his elevated WBC, dyspnea, diffuse wheezing on exam, we felt he was having a COPD exacerbation. We treated with levofloxacin and prednison with a planned five day course ___. Upon initiating this treatment, his dyspnea improved significantly and he was breathing comfortably on the day of discharge. # Syncopal event: syncopal event occured on ___ without loss of consciousness, in the setting of straining for a bowel movement. With a question of possible head strike, we obtained a CT which was negative. EKG showed a. fib without RVR. Electrolytes WNL, serum tox negative, urine tox negative. It was felt to be a vagal event. He had no further events during his hospitalization. # Homelessness: Patient reportedly did not take his torsemide because he was unable to have it filled. I am concerned that his acess to medications is the primary factor resulting in his readmission. Social work saw him this admission. Prior to discharge, we filled an entire month of his medications. He had a place to stay with the help of his sister. # CAD s/p multiple PCI in the past, repeat EKG with poor R wave progression, no evidence of worsening ischemia last cath ___ without intervention. Continued aspirin 81 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, and heart failure as above. # AFib: CHADS score at least 2. Continue home rivaroxaban and carvedilol. # Chronic Hep C: Per PMHx has chronic HCV, per OMR, HCV ___ was nondetectable. # COPD: Continued home albuterol/ipratroprium nebs # Hypertension: Continued home carvedilol, lisinopril, imdur # Hyperlipidemia: Continued home atorvastatin Transitional issues: - Levofloxacin and prednisone ___. - Patient given 1 month supply of all meds here. Please coordinate to ensure this patient has access to his medications. - transitional issues: CT in ___ significant for newly appeared right lower lobe nodules, needing CT followup in 6 month
summary: ___ initially came to the hospital because of some increased left foot pain and some concern about increased shortness of breath. We were worried that ___ may have extra fluid in your lungs because ___ had been unable to take your home torsemide. We gave ___ some medications to help ___ pee off fluid. Because of your increased wheezing, we also felt like ___ may have a COPD exacerbation, which we are treating with antibiotics and steroidsYou will need to takes these medications for three days after discharge to complete a five day course (___). The last day ___ will take the levofloxacin and the prednisone is ___. Please use your inhaler during that time also. We also asked our social worker to see ___ to connect ___ with resources.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is ___ year old woman with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, cardiomyopathy (thought due to both ischemic and non-ischemic disease), and systolic CHF with an EF of 25% who presented with dyspnea on exertion. Telemetry was significant for wide complex tachycardia. # Wide complex tachycardia: Likely atrial fibrillation with abberrancy. The patient was on amiodarone (200mg daily) as an outpatient - this was increased to 200mg BID upon arrival but returned back to daily prior to discharge. She was continued on metoprolol, but this was decreased to 25mg daily due to lower blood pressure (SBP90-100s). The patient underwent TEE and cardioversion on ___, which converted her back to sinus rhythm. Unfortunately, on ___, patient converted back to atrial fibrillation, which triggered episodes of slow ventricular tachycardia. Because of this, the decision was made to perform atrioventricular junction ablation on ___. As for anticoagulation, pradaxa was discontinued due to her low CrCl (<15). She was started on coumadin with heparin drip as a bridge. # Acute systolic CHF: Recent echo with EF <20%. Cardiomyopathy thought to be ischemic and non-ischemic. Etiology of acute exacerbation likely secondary to tachycardia (atrial fibrillation and ventricular tachycardia) which has been causing dysynchrony. Patient appeared grossly volume overloaded on admission. She was diuresed with lasix 80mg IV BID. This was later transitioned to lasix 80mg PO daily when euvolemic. She was also started on spironolactone 25mg. Metoprolol was continued. Per the patient, lisinopril 10mg was recently discontinued. This is likely secondary to ___. Would recommend restarting lisinopril as an outpatient once her Cr stabilizes. Discharge weight 50.7kg. # CAD: Patient was chest pain free. Continued aspirin and simvastatin. # Acute on chronic kidney disease: Stage IV, baseline Cr 1.8 in ___ per PCP ___. Admission Cr 3.1. This is likely secondary to poor forward flow in the setting of CHF. Cr trended down with diuresis. # Hypothyroidism: Continue home synthroid. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: * Patient started on coumadin for anticoagulation. Please check INR on ___ and daily until therapeutic. * Consider restarting an ACE-inhibitor once Cr stabilizes given CHF * Patient discharged to rehab. Her family would like the patient to live with them. However, if she returns home, the patient was informed that she should not be driving.
summary: As you recall, you were admitted because you were having difficulty breathing. This was because your heart beat was irregular and fast, which caused your heart to not pump as efficiently. You underwent a procedure called cardioversion to attempt to put your heart back in a regular rhythm. Unfortunately, your heart continued to be irregular. Because of this, you underwent a second procedure, called atrioventricular junction ablation. Your heart rate will now be paced. We also treated you with medications to get the fluid out of your lungs. Because of your irregular heart beat, we started you on coumadin to prevent clot formation and stroke. You will need to get your blood level (INR) checked periodically to make sure the coumadin dose is correct.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year-old female with AS s/p AVR on warfarin, CHF, tachy-brady s/p PPM, CKD, AF on Coumadin, presenting with acute blood loss anemia concerning for GI bleed in the context of a supratherapeutic INR. GIB stopped spontaneously, without intervention. The patient was also found to be volume overloaded after RBC/FFP resuscitation, and diuresed prior to discharge. ACTIVE ISSUES: =================================================== # GI bleed with acute blood loss anemia: Known history of bleeding angioectasia; presented with melena in the setting of a supratherapeutic INR. Time course over the previous few days had no stigmata of a rapid bleed and she was hemodynamically stable. She was transfused with 1u PRBCs, 1 u FFP and given IV pantoprazole 40 mg q12h upon initial transfer to the MICU. A GI consult was obtained that recommended no endoscopy. GIB stopped spontaneously shortly after admission. Hemoglobin was stable at time of discharge. Vital signs remained normal during hospitalization. INR management as below. # Supratherapeutic INR: 6.6 on arrival (target INR listed in OMR = 2.8-3.0); she received 1 u FFP in the ED. Warfarin was held and INR allowed to normalize. No vitamin K given. When INR trended down to 2.9, 1.5 mg warfarin given, before resuming home regimen of 1 mg daily. Please refer to anticoagulation worksheet for further details. CHRONIC ISSUES: ==================================================== # Atrial fibrillation. Coumadin was held for supratherapeutic INR. She was treated with Metoprolol Tartrate 12.5 q6h for rate control and her home dose of digoxin 0.0625 mg was also continued. # Aortic stenosis: s/p St. ___ mechanical AVR. Anticoagulated with warfarin; target INR listed as ___ in OMR. Warfarin was held for supratherapeutic INR, as above. # Acute on chronic systolic and diastolic heart failure (EF 45%, ___: diuretics initially were held in the setting of GI bleed. Transfusion of 1 u pRBC & 1 u FFP, in light of holding diuretics, led to mild hypervolemia, as evidenced by elevated JVD and orthopnea, though no O2 requirement or peripheral edema. Diuresed with IV furosemide. Home torsemide 10 mg daily restarted and weight remained stable. -- Weight on discharge: 47.4 kg. ===================================================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ===================================================== - ANTICOAGULATION: reevaluate her INR goal for her mechanical AV. Narrow target may lead to supratherapeutic INR. Consider more frequent INR checks, or at home monitoring. - CHF: DISCHARGE WEIGHT 47.4 kg # CONTACT: HCP: ___ (daughter)- ___ # CODE: Full code (confirmed with HCP)
summary: You were seen in our hospital because you had blood in your stools. We also found that your levels of blood thinner were too high, which may have contributed to your bleeding. We gave you one unit of blood and one unit of plasma to help with your bleeding and blood thinner levels. After one night in the Intensive Care Unit, your blood counts improved, and we were able to move you to a regular medical bed, without needing a procedure to stop the bleeding. Your blood counts remained stable, and by the time you went home your blood thinner levels were within your normal range. You were also found to have some extra fluid on your lungs. We treated this by giving you higher doses of your water pill through the IV. By the time you left, your lungs were much more clear.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mrs. ___ is a ___ y/o woman with PMH of sarcoidosis, AF s/p AV ablation and PPM, dCHF, IDDM presenting with worsening SOB of unclear etiology despite extensive work-up. At discharge, her respiratory status was much improved but given persistent DOE, she was discharged with home supplemental oxygen as well as follow-up with outpatient Cardiology and Pulmonary for continued work-up. ACUTE PROBLEMS: # Dyspnea on exertion: On admission, patient presented with acute on chronic SOB. Differential for possible source of SOB/DOE included cardiac, pulmonary, infectious, vascular (PE), and rheumatologic. Given normal troponins and unchanged EKG with recent stress test showing normal cardiac perfusion ___, ischemic was felt to be unlikely. TTE showed symmetric LVH, enlarged LA but preserved EF with only mild MR and moderate TR without PFO. These suggested against appreciable worsening of cardiomyopathy. Furthermore, lack of significant TR suggested against pulmonary hypertension. D-dimer was initially elevated, but subsequent V/Q scan revealed low likelihood for PE, especially given patient's therapeutic anticoagulation status. CXR and high-resolution CT chest was not suggestive of acute intra-pulmonary process such as pulmonary fibrosis or sarcoid flare. Regarding infectious etiology, the patient never had a fever or other suggestive signs. Lastly, rheumatologic was entirely negative including ANCA, SPEP/UPEP, HIV, ___, RF, anti-scl, and anti-jo1. For management, patient was supported with supplemental O2 and inhalers. She was treated for sinus congestion was below and diuresed maximally with both PO and IV lasix. Given ambulatory de-saturations in O2, she was ultimately prescribed home O2 on discharge, at which point her respiratory status had improved but was still notable for persistent DOE. During this hospitalization, the patient was followed closely by Pulmonary and set-up for continued follow-up as an outpatient. Overall, her symptoms are likely multifactorial with generalized de-conditioning ___ chronic inactivity a major contributor. # Afib s/p AV junctional ablation with ___ pacemaker implantation: The patient was followed closely on telemetry during this admission without any significant events noted. She was continued on home warfarin with INR kept between goal of ___. Her INR at discharged was 2.1. # dCHF: The patient has a history of HFpEF and her cardiac function was evaluated as above, noted to be similar as compared to TTE in ___. She was diuresed with goal of optimizing respiratory status and at discharge her dry weight was found to be 86.2kg. # Headaches: During this hospitalization, the patient did have c/o headaches and sinus congestion. She was evaluated with a CT head NC on ___ showing no acute intracranial process with possible unilateral sinus mucosal thickening, unlikely to be bacterial sinusitis. She was treated with intra-nasal saline and fluticasone for symptomatic management. CHRONIC PROBLEMS: # Chronic pancreatitis: She was continued on home Creon. # NIDDM: The patient was not on any medication at home and she was managed on n ISS while in the hospital. # Hypothyroidism: She was continued on home levothyroxine. # HTN: She was continued on home atenolol. She was briefly on lisinopril for additional BP control but this medication was not needed upon discharge. # HLD: She was continued on home Simvastatin TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -Please follow up with Dr. ___ after discharge and obtain pulmonary function tests -Patient will need outpatient sleep study following discharge -Please follow up with Cardiology following discharge -New medications: Fluticasone Propionate NASAL 2 SPRY NU BID and Lisinopril 5 mg PO DAILY -Patient being discharged with home supplemental oxygen -Weight upon discharge: 86.2kg -Repeat CBC in 1 week to re-assess leukocytosis
summary: You were admitted because of worsening shortness of breath, which was concerning for a problem with your heart. ___ had testing, which showed that your heart function was unchanged and likely not causing your symptomsYou were also seen by the Pulmonary team (lung doctors), who did not find any obvious lung disease to explain your symptoms. In order to improve your shortness of breath, you were given inhalers, supplemental oxygenation, diuretics (water pills) to help remove any extra fluid from body, and medications to better control your blood pressure. At discharge, ___ were feeling better in terms of your breathing.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ obese F with dCHF, asthma, HTN and atrial fibrillation (on coumadin and verapamil) who presents with worsening SOB and fluid retention. . #. SOB/Edema: Patient presented with symptoms consistent with CHF exacerbation. She was treated with multiple prn doses of 40mg IV furosemide and had excellent response with fluid output. Her lower extremity edema improved. We continued to check her electrolyte status during diuresis. Her creatinine levels remained within normal ranges during diuresis. On admission she was very SOB and required 4L of 02 satting at 95%. She was maintained on oxygen for several days. She was also treated with ipratroprium nebulizers PRN for SOB. By discharge, she was satting 93% on 1 L and 86% on RA, so was d/c on ___ L home O2. The patient also had an Echocardiogram performed to evaluate her heart function and establish a possible cause of this acute exacerbation of CHF. Patient had a previous echo but results were limited due to body habitus. New echo showed global hypokinesis and an ejection fraction of ___. Due to her CHF, patient was started on lisinopril 5mg daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily, increased to metoprolol succinate 150mg daily, and started on torsemide 40mg daily. She should continue with these medications as an outpatient. Ms. ___ has an appointment to follow up with her PCP, ___, on ___ ___ at 8:30am. Dr. ___ was informed of all of the changes made to Ms. ___ regimen during her stay. Patient should also follow up with her cardiologist, Dr. ___, on ___ at 2:40. Dr. ___ has also been notified about her hospital course. She was discharged on ___ on home O2, with stable VS. Metoprolol 75mg TID was changed to Toprol XL 200mg daily for ease of dosing and she was also d/c on torsemide 20mg daily. . #. Asthma: Patient had minimal wheezing throughout her stay but was maintained on prn Albuterol nebs for intermittent SOB. Patient should continue on home albuterol inhaler after dishcarge. . #. Atrial fibrillation: Patient was found to be in afib per EKG. Not symptomatic. Patient was maintained on coumadin with therepeutic INR. Home dose of coumadin was changed from 5mg 5x/week and 2.5mg 2x/week to 5mg daily. Her home verapimil was DCed and metoprolol was substituted due to better protection from CHF. Patient should continue these medications at home. . #. EtOH: Patient drinks ___ drinks per night. CAGE (-). No signs of withdrawal during her stay. CIWA scale was calculated to be less than 10. No ativan was given. She was counseled on the contraindications of drinking to her extent, especially related to her heart health. Patient was informed that alcohol use might be contributing to her worsening heart failure, and should discontinue alcohol in the future. She should follow up with her PCP to discuss this in more detail. . #. Anxiety/depression: Patient was maintained on home dose of sertraline and had no symptoms. Patient should continue on sertraline at home.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for increasing shortness of breath and fluid retention that was not being alleviated with no resolution at home with PO lasix. You were treated in the hospital with multiple doses of IV furosemide. You had excellent output of urine in response to the IV furosemide. Your leg edema was much improved. You also received an Echocardiogram of your heart to try to interpret why you had this acute exacerbation of water retention. It looks like you have worsening heart failure since your last echo was performed in ___. We added some new medications which will help to protect your heart in the setting of congestive heart failure. You should also stop your intake of alcohol to help improve your heart failure, as this is likely contributing.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo woman with HFpEF (40-50%), permanent afib (rate controlled on amiodarone), CKDIII, HTN, and hypothyroidism admitted for exertional dyspnea, elevated BNP (___), and pulmonary edema on CXR that was most consistent with acute on chronic heart failure exacerbation. Negative troponins, CBC wnl, INR 2.0, and negative UA. UCx with mixed flora c/w contamination. BCx pending on discharge. #Acute on Chronic Heart Faiulre: Precipitant of acute exacerbation felt to be acute elevation in blood pressure (145-162 systolic on admission). As the patient was not on any anti-hypertensives, we discharged her on 10 mg of Lisinopril that can be uptitrated with appropriate electrolyte monitoring as an outpatient. Patient did not endorse any medication non-adherence or dietary indiscretions. She did endorse a new mildly productive cough for 2 days prior to admission, but no fever and normal WBC. The patient improved symptomatically after IV Lasix 40 mg x 5 over 3 days with an ambulatory O2 sat 96. K/Mg were repleted as appropriate. She was net even on 40mg PO QD of Lasix before discharge. #Hypertension: As the patient had BP>140s systolic >2x on admission with report of variable BPs at home, we discharged her on Lisinopril 10 mg, which can be uptitrated as an outpatient. We felt this was a good first-line BP medication given concurrent CKD. We did not think this was white coat hypertension because her blood pressure tended to predictably increase to the 140s-50s in the ___ and early AM before her next dose of Lisinopril. Her Cr bump was within expected range after starting ACEi at 5 mg (1.4-->1.6) and then discharging her on 10 mg (Cr 1.4). #Permanent atrial fibrillation: Patient was monitored on tele and continued to be in rate-controlled atrial fibrillation on admission on amiodarone and apixaban. She has had many cardioversions in the past and underwent DCCV on ___ as an inpatient, which was successful and put her back in sinus rhythm. She has an appointment to follow-up with cardiologist Dr. ___. # CKD: Patient was around baseline Cr ~1.5 during admission and discharge Cr was 1.4 # Asthma: continue albuterol q6h PRN: shortness of breath # Hypothyroidism: continue levothyroxine 50 mcg 5x/week; 100 mcg 2x/week # Ventral hernia: Has been evaluated as outpatient; did not have signs of obstruction or incarceration during this admission. # GERD: continued home ranitidine 75 mg daily Transitional Issues: New Meds: Lisinopril 10mg daily Diuretic Regimen: Lasix 40 mg PO daily CODE: FULL CONTACT: Daughter, ___, ___ ___ weight: 83.7kg
summary: You were admitted for feeling short of breath when you were walking, which we attributed to a build up of fluid in your lungs due to your heart not pumping blood forward as well as it can. For this, we gave you an IV medication to take some fluid off your body called "Lasix" or "Furosemide". Your weight on admission was 189.1 lbs and your weight on discharge was 184. We discharged you on a home dose of Lasix of 40mg. Please take this daily to help prevent fluid building up again. There are many causes of the heart not working as well as it should. We suspect that your elevated blood pressures might be contributing, in part, to your heart not working as well as it should. Therefore, we started you on a blood pressure medication called Lisinopril, which also can help improve your kidney function in the long-term.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ woman with a history of atrial fibrillation, DVT (on Coumadin, INR 3.3 but ranging 1.9-9), CHF, recent R humerus fracture, referred from clinic for DOE, atypical CP, low O2 sat. & new O2 req. concerning for hypoxic resp. failure, found to have PE on CT-PA (likely chronic) and chronic LLE DVT, and pulmonary edema concerning for CHF exacerbation in the setting of missing her home Lasix. # Acute hypoxic respiratory failure: Likely multifactorial in the setting of pulmonary edema, resolving pneumonia, and age-indeterminate DVT/PE (likely chronic). See below for further details on management. # Acute on chronic diastolic CHF (LVEF 65-70%) in the setting of holding home Lasix. Evidence of pulmonary edema on CXR and pulmonary hypertension on recent OSH TTE. She received IV Lasix for diuresis with good UOP (down 2L and 1 kg from 2 days prior). She became clinically euvolemic and weaned her oxygen. We restarted her home 80 mg PO Lasix. # Recent HCAP, resolved prior to this admission. Treated adequately at outside hospital (OSH) with IV antibiotics. Afebrile, normal WBC count so antibiotics were deferred. Unlikely to see resolution of infiltrates this early after treatment. # History of DVT/PE (x3), now provoked # Chronic VTE Found to have PE on CT-PA (likely chronic), and chronic LLE DVT on ___ in the setting of recent hospital admission for humeral fracture and lack of mobility over previous month. Previously had a DVT in college while on OCPs and again during pregnancy. Started on warfarin in ___ after her fem-pop bypass. We continued her warfarin as above. TTE showed evidence a mildly dilated, mildly hypokinetic right ventricle. # Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation: Found during recent OSH admission (___) to be in new-onset afib with RVR (x 8 days with HR 180, converted to sinus with HR ___. We continue her warfarin as above and monitored her on telemetry while inpatient. # Atypical chest pain: Recent positive stress test at ___, ___ x 2 neg. EKG with no ischemic changes. We recommended outpatient cardiology f/u and cath. # Insulin-dependent type 2 diabetes: Recent hospitalization for symptomatic severe hypoglycemia with hypoglycemia unawareness. We consulted the ___ Diabetes team, who recommended glargine qhs at a lower dose (18 U) while inpatient, Humalog for carb counting (TID with meals) while inpatient, and ___ f/u appointment. # Right humerus fracture: Ortho and OT were consulted. Repeat XRs show comminuted fracture in the proximal third of the right humerus. RUE (nonweightbearing in sling) was switched to a collar provided by OT. She needs ___ clinic f/u and a pre-op cardiology eval if there is a plan for surgery. # Vitamin D insufficiency: We started her on Vitamin D ___ IU daily # Subclinical hypothyroidism: identified on admission labs. We recommend rechecking TSH in 2 months as outpatient. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ===================== -INR 1.9 on discharge. She was discharged on Lovenox (for bridging) and Coumadin 5 mg daily. Her next INR should be drawn on ___ and her Coumadin dose should be adjusted as needed. -Patient needs outpatient cardiology follow-up and likely cardiac catheterization for abnormal stress test at ___ ___. -Patient is scheduled to follow-up with orthopedics in two weeks; she should remain non-weightbearing in the sling (shoulder pendulum exercises okay). -Discharged on Lasix 80 mg po daily. Weight on discharge: 82.9 kg. Cr on discharge: 0.8. -Insulin regimen was changed due to hypoglycemia. Lantus dose decreased to 18. She received Humalog 5 units with meals and a HISS. ___ follow-up arranged as outpatient. -Admission labs notable for subclinical hypothyroidism; PCP should repeat TSH in two months. -Vitamin D ___ daily started for vitamin D insufficiency. -would recommend cardiology evaluation for cardiac cath prior to any surgical procedure
summary: You were admitted from clinic due to difficulty breathing while walking and low oxygen levels. A chest x-ray showed fluid in your lungs, as well as a resolving pneumonia. You were treated with IV Lasix and your breathing/oxygen levels improved. A CT scan of your chest showed a blood clot in your lungs, which is most likely old. In addition, an ultrasound of your legs showed a blood clot in your legs, also most likely old. It is very important that you continue to take Coumadin as prescribed. Because of your positive stress test at ___, it is very important that you see a cardiologist as an outpatient for evaluation and to schedule a cardiac catheterization. The ___ diabetes doctor helped manage your blood sugar while you were in the hospital. We have scheduled you an appointment to see ___ as an outpatient (see below). The orthopedics team and occupational therapist evaluated your humerus fracture. The splint was removed and replaced with a special sling.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ a PMH of systolic CHF (EF 20% ___, atrial fibrillation on rivaroxaban, CVA without residual defects, pulmonary embolism in ___, HTN, DM, CVA, and HBV who presents from his PCP's office with dyspnea on exertion, found to have a pleural effusion and decompensated CHF. # Acute on chronic systolic dysfunction: EF 20% ___. Presentation consistent with decompenstaed heart failure. Adm wt of 96.8 much increased from weight at last discharge of 90.9kg(reportedly before completing diuresis). BNP elevated on admission. JVP elevated to 13cm, 2+ edema bilaterally and pt with massive R pleural effusion and pulmonary edema. Trigger is most likely dietary indiscretion(pt reporting eating pizza/hamburgers) and med non-complicance(pt reports intermittently taking lasix). Per med rec, has not been taking spirnolactone and digoxin. Was dig loaded and restarted on spirnolactone. Started on lasix gtt with very good UO. Left AMA while still volume overloaded. UO very well to lasix gtt. # R pleural effusion: Chronic R pleural effusion since re-presenting in ___. Had ___ for the R pleural effusion at ___ during recent hospitalization from ___ that was transudative. AFPX3 negative. Had chest tube in. Drained >2L, had subsequent severe cough in the setting of rexpansion pulm edema, chest tube removed per pt request. Pleural fluid consistent with transudative process, no organisms in pleural fluid. # Atrial fibrillation: Pt with history of Afib. Afib w/ RVR during early course of hospitalization to 120-140s. Pt intermittently refused medication complicating HR control. Home dose metop 150mg XL increased to 200mg XL. We were unable to secure authorization for rivaroxaban (his outside providers have been working on this) and the patient left before we were able to obtain this medication for him. He had three rivaroxaban pills, but will need a refill on ___. He understood the importance of taking this medication and agreed to contact his PCP about finishing the authorization. # Discharge against medical advice(AMA) : The patient left AMA despite an understanding of the risk of worsening symptoms, repeat hospitalization and/or death. The primary team underscored the importance of completing diuresis to avoid further hospitalizations or potenial death from volume overload. Primary team also discussed need for cardiac catherization to explore and manage potential coronary obstruction leading to ischemic cardiomyopathy. Patient verbalized understanding, and reported that he was ok with taking the risk and left against medical advice. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: [ ] will need assistance with obtaining rivaroxaban (prior authorization started by PCP) as we were unable to complete this prior to his leaving AMA [ ] would benefit from cardiac catheterization [ ] if pleural effusion continues to recur, he would likely benefit from pleurx [ ] he was reloaded on digoxin [ ] please recheck INR as jumped to 3.4 on day of discharge [ ] please titrate blood pressure medications: - metoprolol increased to succinate 200mg - hydralazine was decreased to 10mg TID (from 20mg) given soft bps - lisinopril decreased to 20mg (from 40mg) [ ] pleural fluid culture and cytology pending [ ] Discharge weight: 88.9 - patient left AMA
summary: You were admitted on ___ with shortness of breath and were found to be in worsening heart failure. You were given IV medication to help remove fluid from your lungs. You also had a chest tube placed to drain fluid from your chest. This caused you to cough so you wanted it removed. We also thought you would benefit from a procedure called cardiac catheterization to look at the blood vessels that feed your heart. You declined this procedure. We think you still have more volume in your lungs and body that need to be removed and that you should stay in the hospital to continue receiving IV medications. You decided that you wanted to leave the hospital against our medical advice. You understood the risks of leaving but still chose to leave.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female with history of atrial fibrillation on apixiban s/p admission for ___ with cardioversion ___, presenting with syncope and dyspnea. # Syncope: Likely secondary to known orthostatic hypotension w/ similar prior episodes in the past. Patient reported poor PO intake prior to admission w/ elevated creat and BUN/creat ratio. Troponins neg, unlikely to be PE as patient is anticoagulated. No arrhythmias on telemetry to explain syncope. Patient evaluated by autonomic Neurology service who recommended further w/u of underlying etiology. Her B12 was considered borderline low so she was started on PO B12. Her ___, SPEP, TSH, Ro, and La were normal. Additionally there a concern for possible pheochromocytoma so workup was started but was still pending at discharge. They recommended continuing midodrine (should not take a bedtime dose, due to risk of supine hypertension), increased PO intake, physical therapy, and further outpatient assessment. Patient will need outpatient autonomic testing. In addition, deconditioning may have exacerbated orthostatic hypotension. Physical therapy should be carried daily if possible, i.e., walking or standing with support. Brief periods of standing followed by sitting when symptomatic will help with reconditioning. Reclining bike and water exercises (with careful supervision) may also be helpful in the outpatient setting. #Flecanaide Toxicity: During hospital course there was concern for Flecainide toxicity due to initial prolonged QT then an episode of Ventricular Tachycardia. Flecainide was stopped She was started on Aldactone and Normal Saline to counteract the Na channel blockade. She was given IV Lasix to try to keep her net even. She was additionally stopped on her TCA. She has follow up with EP in 1 month. QTc on ___ was 406. #ATRIAL FIBRILLATION: in SR s/p DC/CV ___ and flecainide initiation. On admission patient in sinus bradycardia with prolonged QTc. Discontinued Amitriptyline. Decreased dose of metoprolol to 12.5mg BID (hold for HR<45) and continued Flecainide. QTc normalized, patient with no concerning events on telemetry, in SR at 50-72. Patient to f/u with EP/cards outpatient. # Acute systolic on chronic diastolic CHF (new systolic dysfuncion, known diastolic)- admission, mild shortness of breath, worse with exertion, for weeks concerning for heart failure. She was diuresed with lasix prn. A repeat echo was obtained given concerns for flecainide toxicity which showed a newly depressed EF of 35%, thought to be due to takatsubo's vs. flecainide induced cardiomyopathy. A final echo was obtained on day of discharge ___ which showed improved RV function but stable LVEF of 35%. Patient was adequately diuresed and asymptomatic by time of discharge. Discharge weight was 67.8kg. # Depression- lorazemam prn. # Anemia Macrocytic. Stable. No e/o active bleeding. Folate, Ferritin WNL. B12 was borderline low, started PO repletion and f/u outpatient. Her spep, upep neg ***TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:*** - f/u with PCP after discharge - f/u with autonomics neurology outpatient - patient should follow up with Dr ___ in 1 month - needs repeat Echo prior to Cards appt - f/u metanephrines (sent to rule out pheochromocytoma given BP lability and new heart failiure - PCP to determine need for B12 repletion (level was 376) - Patient may benefit from brief periods of standing followed by sitting when symptomatic will help with reconditioning. Reclining bike and water exercises (with careful supervision) may also be helpful in the outpatient setting.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with syncope. We were concerned that this was related to your heart, so you were monitored on the cardiology service. Your syncope was likely related to orthostatic hypotension (drop in your blood pressure and dizziness when you stand). You were seen by the neurologists who specialize in this, who they recommended physical therapy, increase intake of fluid to prevent dehydration, and wearing compression stockings. We also sent additional tests to try to determine the cause of your neuropathy, some of which are still pending. You were found to have borderline low vitamin B12 levels, so you were started on supplements.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY: ___ with history of AS (mean gradient 34mmHg), TIA, carotid stenosis, suspected MDS ___ deferred), chronic left leg swelling, and chronic back pain presents with fatigue and malaise, likely due to pulmonary edema from heart failure. # Diastolic Heart Failure | Hypoxemia The patient was admitted with symptoms of general sense of malaise, not feeling well, and hypoxemia. Labs of elevated proBNP and chest x-ray of pulmonary edema suggested diastolic heart failure as the main etiology. His weight of 59 kg was up from his most recent PCP office weight of 57 kg. He had a TTE that showed worsening AS to moderate severity and new elevated estimated PA pressure. PE was considered as a less likely possibility but he did have a non-specific positive d-dimer. He was initiated on diuresis with bumetanide (listed allergy to furosemide) with good response. However, he developed ___ with diuresis so further doses of bumetanide were held. Despite appearing clinically euvolemic, he still had hypoxemia with 2L O2 requirement. A V/Q scan was performed, which showed low probability of PE. With increased activity level and time, his O2 requirement resolved, suggesting that ultimately volume overload was the main contributor. Discharge weight was 55.7 kg. # 2nd Degree Mobitz Type 1 Heart Block On admission, telemetry showed that patient had intermittent heart block, which was confirmed on EKG to be type ___lock. His heart rate dropped to ___ when sleeping, but when awake, he was completely asymptomatic. He never developed higher levels of AV block. Given no symptoms, no intervention was pursued other than stopping his home beta blocker. # Left Knee Pain The patient developed an acutely swollen and painful left knee after initiation of diuresis. It was thought to be most likely due to pseudogout vs gout. He did not have any exam findings to suggest infection. The main findings were only a palpable effusion. The joint was aspirated and 40mg of Depo-Medrol was injected. The fluid analysis unfortunately did not confirm any crystals. His knee pain shortly resolved with no ongoing symptoms while inpatient. # Hypertension: Held metoprolol as discussed above. # CKD: Baseline 1.6-2.1, was 1.6 in ___. Continued calcitriol. # Carotid Stenosis: Continued aspirin and cholesterol meds. # Hyperlipidemia: Continued fish oil and atorvastatin. # Chronic Pain: Continued acetaminophen and tramadol prn. # BPH: Continued finasteride. # Suspected MDS: Continued B12 supplement. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Patient's metoprolol was discontinued on discharge due to intermittent high degree 2nd degree type 1 AV block. He should have cardiology follow up for both his diastolic heart failure as well as the node block. - On discharge, he was not given any diuretics due to rising creatinine that developed with diuresis. The ___ resolved at discharge, but given the high risk for recurrence, he was not discharged on diuretics. Please re-evaluate this as an outpatient. His discharge dry weight is 55.7kg.
summary: You were admitted to ___ after presenting here with feeling fatigued. We did a work up and found that you likely had a heart failure exacerbation from having too much fluid on board. We gave you medications to help you pee, which ultimately improved your oxygenation and your symptoms. While here, you developed acute left knee pain, which we think was due to either gout or pseudogout, an inflammatory condition. We injected your knee with steroids, which helped improve the pain and swelling. We also found that your heart rate gets very slow at times. While this is not a dangerous condition, we recommend stopping your metoprolol, which can cause slow heart rate. Last, please weigh yourself everyday.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with h/o recent STEMI in ___ c/b GI bleed (EGD negative) and CHF (EF 47%) who p/w 1 week of worsening atraumatic R shoulder pain and admitted for incidental finding of hgb drop and c/f GIB. # Right shoulder pain: Likely musculoskeletal from longstanding osteoarthritis. Shoulder X-ray with severe glenohumeral joint osteoarthritis. Patient was continued on her home Tylenol with 1x need for tramadol in ED. Initially seen by ___ in ED given c/f inability to use walker, however on arrival to floor, patient was able to range her arm fully. ___ reeval recommended her to return home with home ___. # ANEMIA: Iron studies during previous hospitalization suggestive of anemia of chronic disease and Fe deficiency; at that time EGD was normal and without evidence of bleeding. Patient was monitored here o/n; hgb was stable. Patient was started on PO iron supplementation given microcytic anemia. Iron studies and hemolysis labs were pending at d/c. Patient was continued on home lansoprazole. Discussed with patient and patient's daughter, ___, role of colonoscopy in further assessing anemia. Patient to discuss with PCP the risks and benefits of colonoscopy. # systolic heart failure, EF 47%: Patient appears to have no acute decompensation at this time, although in ED there was some c/f slightly worsened ___ edema. Lasix recently increased to 60mg daily in outpatient setting. BNP 5693 at this admission from 30K. CXR w/ mild pulmonary edema, stable cardiomegaly, probable small pleural effusions. Patient given 20mg IV Lasix in ED. Patient was subsequently continued on home Furosemide 60 mg PO DAILY. Continued Losartan Potassium 25 mg PO DAILY, and Metoprolol Succinate XL 100 mg PO DAILY. Discharge [bed] weight was 80.5kg. # CAD, s/p recent STEMI: No recent or current CP. EKG unchanged from prior. Continued home Nitroglycerin SL 0.4 mg SL Q5MIN:PRN chest pain, home Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY, home Atorvastatin 80 mg PO QPM, home Clopidogrel 75 mg PO DAILY. # GERD: continued home lansoprazole. # CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE: Creatinine noted to be in the range of 1.4-2.0. 1.7 on admission. Creatinine 1.7 at discharge. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES -would strongly recommend colonoscopy for evaluation of possible source of bleeding; please discuss with PCP and help arrange this -started on iron supplementation; please monitor for constipation -for severe right shoulder pain related to osteoarthritis, would consider intraarticular steroids for symptom relief # CODE: FULL # CONTACT: ___ (daughter) ___
summary: You were admitted to the ___ for anemia (low blood level), which you have had in prior. You were observed overnight and had repeat labs which showed stable blood counts. It is likely that your blood levels are low because of chronic disease, low iron, in addition to very slow oozing from your gastrointestinal tract. We noted that you had an upper endoscopy during your prior hospitalization; this did not show any evidence of active bleed. Therefore, we think it is possible that you might be having ongoing, slow losses from your colon (which is not uncommon if you have polyps). We recommend further discussions with your primary care doctor to assess the risks and benefits of getting a colonoscopy.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yoF with history CAD, AF and ___ presents with SOB and weight gain in the setting of medication changes, initially thought to be in afib with RVR, but was later found to be in atrial tachycardia. The patient was also found to be in moderate acute on chronic heart failure. . #Heart failure: The patient was found to have crackles in her lungs and symptoms consistent with heart failure. An echo showed that she had a decreased ejection fraction (30%) from her previous baseline of 40-45%. Initial read of the echo was of wall motion abnormalities that were consistent with a coronary territory, however, after re-read by Dr. ___ felt that the heart failure was more consistent with heart failure ___ atrial tachycardia. The patient was titrated upwards on her metoprolol and furosemide with subsequent improvement of her symptoms by discharge. . #Coronary artery disease: The patient underwent nuclear stress testing, which showed a reversible septal defect. She then proceeded to cardiac catherization, which showed near total occlusion of her LAD with distal filling from collaterals, as well as a 90% stenosis of her RCA, which was stented with a BMS. She was discharged on aspirin and plavix. . #Atrial tachycardia: The patient underwent ablation of her tachycardia focus on ___. During the procedure however, the patient had a drug-induced asystolic event. The patient was paced and a permanent pacemaker was implanted. The patient then was up-titrated on metoprolol and amiodarone loading was started. She was discharged with an amiodarone taper and metoprolol. Coumadin was not started due to the patient's past history of massive GI bleed. . #Acute on chronic renal failure: The patient was found to be in mild acute renal failure on admission, worsened from her prior creatinine baseline of 1.8. She was given mucomyst prior to and after her cardiac catherization, and her renal function returned to near baseline at discharge with creatinine of 2.0. . #Hematocrit drop: The patient experienced an acute drop in her hematocrit of 10 points after her ablation procedure, and was found to have bilateral groin hematomas on exam. She was transfused one unit of blood. Her hematocrit remained stable thereafter for the remainder of her stay.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had a fast heart rate and difficulty breathing. We found that your heart was beating at an abnormally fast rate, in a rhythm called atrial tachycardia. For this, we treated you by ablating the part of your heart that was causing the abnormal rhythm, and implanted a pacemaker to help stop your heart from going too slowly. Also, a picture of your heart called an echocardiogram was taken and found that your heart was only pumping at 30% efficiency (normal is 55%-60%). Because of this, we felt that you might have an artery that was blocked. We found that this was indeed the case when you had a cardiac catherization, and a stent was placed in one of the arteries of your heart to improve blood flow to your heart and help it function better. Finally, while you were here in the hospital, we noticed that your blood counts dropped immediately after your catherizations. We found that you developed some bleeding in the groin around where your catherizations were performed. You received a transfusion because of this. Your bleeding stopped and your blood counts stabilized.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M with CHF (EF 20%), CAD s/p MI, p/w hypotension, malaise, and dry cough, treated for CAP with 10 day course of levofloxacin. Shock of Unknown Etiology - Patient presented to the ED for 1 week of malaise & non-productive cough which is worse at night. In the ED, patient became hypotensive with systolic BP in the ___ that was refractory to 2L fluids, so patient was transferred to the MICU. Put on levophed drip in ED, but did not require the drip in the MICU. BP on arrival to MICU was 102/48 and his MAP has ranged between 50 and 65. In searching for the cause of his hypotension, the patient has significant systolic heart failure confirmed by TTE revealing an ejection fraction of 20%. AMI unlikely due to no changes in EKG from baseline (Sinus rhythm; q-wave in III, V2, V3, ST ele in Vi, V2, V3, flattening of T waves). Patient was on multiple home anti-hypertensives which were held in the MICU (metoprolol 25 QD, amiodarone 200mg QD and furosemide 20mg QD). Food/liquid intake has been appropriate in the weeks leading up to admission as per patient report. Patient had an initial WBC of 12.7 (trended down over admission), unimpressive U/A, nonproductive cough x 1 week in the setting of generalized fatigue/weakness. A rise in creatinine to 2.2 (baseline 1.7) which resolved after 2 liters of fluid pointed toward a prerenal/dehydration status. O2 sat 97% 2L NC, desaturing to 91% without O2, upon admission and was O2 sat 94% 4L NC on transfer from the MICU. Urine output remained sufficient at about 40-60 cc/hr, but was given small dose of 10mg furosemide with goal of improving congestion and O2 sats, with little effect. Patient was stable for transfer to the medicine ward on ___. Patient continued to have a dry cough during admission. CXRs showed multiple patchy infiltrates and patient was treated for CAP with 10 days of levofloxacin. It was thought that pulmonary process could be a result of amiodarone toxicity. CT scan was gotten and confirmed isolated patchy infiltrates and ground glass opacity surrounding affected areas. Radiology felt strongly that this was not amio toxicity, which is clinically correlated as patient would not have recovered so quickly if it was due to amio-related fibrotic changes. He was stable on the floor with SBPs in the 100-120s. His home metoprolol was restarted, which he tolerated well. Atrius cardiology recommended holding his amio and lasix. WBC was WNL and O2 sats between 94-96% on RA, even when working with ___, Patient felt well. Upon discharge, hypotension was of unknown etiology but thought to be due to sepsis ___ PNA. . # hand numbness- The patient continued to complain of right arm tingling and numbness throughout admission. NCHCT was negative. No other neurological deficits were detected and we monitored this condition closely throughout admission. Symptoms should be followed-up as an outpatient if they do not further resolve. . #. systolic CHF (EF 20%)- BPs meds were held during hypotension. Metoprolol was restarted on the floor and was tolerated by patient well. Continued to hold lasix and amiodarone, per atrius cards recs. Cards also recommended started low dose losartan as an outpatient (do not recommend ACE-I as patient has dry cough). . # CAD - ECG was stable compared to previous ECGs. ASA 81mg was continued. . # Hyperlipidemia - continued home simvastatin. . # Hypothyroidism - not active, home levothyroxine continued. . # Dispo: home with cane # Code Status: Full Code
summary: You came into the hospital because you were not feeling well and your blood pressure was very low. You went to the intensive care unit because you were not doing well. We got a chest xray and a CT scan of your chest that showed that you had a pneumonia. We were concerned that the amiodarone that you are for your heart was affecting your lungs and causing these problems so we asked the cardiology team to see you and they felt that the problems with your lungs were due to infection, and not due to this medication. You are being treated for your pneumonia with an antibiotic (Levofloxacin) which you will need to take for another 5 days. You are taking it every other day and your next dose should be on ___. We made changes to your medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ year old woman with a history of paroxysmal Afib on dabigatran, ___ disease, labile HTN ___ autonomic dysregulation, TIA, reactive airway disease, diastolic CHF with recent admissions for afib with RVR, diarrhea, pneumonia and L hip fracture s/p repair, who presented with shortness of breath. # Shortness of Breath/Hypoxia: likely secondary to recurrent aspiration and amiodarone toxicity, with a component of volume overload. She required several MICU stays for recurrent hypoxia and desaturation. There was initial concern for multifocal pneumonia, HCAP vs. aspiration. She was empirically treated with Vanc/Cefepime/Azithromycin on admission to the MICU. Vancomycin was discontinued on ___ as MRSA screen was negative. She completed a 7 day course of HCAP coverage. Video swallow was negative for oropharyngeal aspiration, but there was concern for increased secretions and possible mild bulbar dysfunction leading to aspiration. Glycopyrrolate was trialed to attempt to reduce secretions but this led to worsening confusion and was stopped. Neurology was consulted and recommended evaluation by her outpatient neurologist for possible botox injections to control secretions. She was diuresed with IV Lasix boluses, and transitioned to 20mg PO Lasix at discharge due to concern for a component of volume overload given elevated BNP of 4929. Pulmonology and cardiology were consulted and felt that her clinical picture was consistent with amiodarone toxicity. Amiodarone was stopped, and she was started on prednisone. She was also initiated on calcium, vitamin D, and Bactrim given projected prolonged steroid course. Respiratory function improved, and she was maintaining O2 saturation > 95% on room air at time of discharge. # Anemia: The patient had an acute drop in hemoglobin to 6.7 from recent baseline which appears to be around ___. Low haptoglobin, elevated LDH and bilirubin were suggestive of transient hemolysis, which resolved without intervention. Direct Coombs was negative, and peripheral smear revealed only rare schistocytes. Hematology was consulted and felt that hemolysis was secondary to a transient transfusion reaction. Iron studies were suggestive of anemia of chronic disease. B12 level was borderline with elevated methylmalonic acid, suggestive of B12 deficiency. She was started on 1000 mcg of cyanocobalamin daily. # Afib on dabigatran: CHADS2VASC score is 7 (HTN, Age x ___, female, HTN, TIA, CHF). Amiodarone was discontinued given concern for amiodarone toxicity as above. She was started on low dose metoprolol 12.5mg XL daily for rate control. # Transaminitis: Mild, new from prior labs. RUQ US was without abnormalities. Transaminitis resolved without intervention, and was likely secondary to hemolysis given marked elevation of LDH and indirect hyperbilirubinemia. # ___ disease: Continued home carbidopa-levodopa, entecapone, and pramipexole # HTN with labile BP in the setting of autonomic dysfunction ___ ___ disease: Continued home amlodipine # Mood disorder: Continued home sertraline Transitional Issues: - started on prednisone 30mg daily. Also started on calcium, vitamin D, and prophylactic Bactrim while taking prednisone. Will likely require prolonged taper, to be directed by Dr. ___ - discharge on 20mg PO Lasix. Should have electrolytes and creatinine checked on ___ - amiodarone discontinued secondary to pulmonary toxicity - consider botox as an outpatient to treat excess secretions - borderline low B12 level. Methylmalonic acid high so started on high dose B12 supplementation. Intrinsic factor Ab pending at discharge - started on low dose metoprolol for rate control of atrial fibrillation
summary: You came in because you were having problems breathing. We think this was due to aspiration (food going down the wrong way into the lungs), fluid from heart failure, and a side effect of your amiodarone. We stopped your amiodarone and started you on a medication called prednisone, which can help treat the lung problems caused by amiodarone. We are discharging you on predisone, which you should take until you see Dr. ___ in clinic. Since prednisone can also cause you to retain fluid, we are discharging you on a water pill called furosemide. You should have your electrolytes and kidney function checked at your next primary care visit on ___, since these can be affected by furosemide.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo man with history of non-ischemic CMP (EF 50->30%), alcohol and tobacco abuse, AF on Coumadin, HTN, and OSA, presenting with dyspnea, worsening ___ edema, and confusion. # Acute on chronic systolic non ischemic CHF exacerbation: Patient has a history of non-ischemic (neg cath ___ CM with EF 30%, ___ class I per history, now at least class II. Patient's recently depressed EF is suspected to be due to alcohol. Pt appearred overloaded on exam and on CXR, BNP 6826 (none prior in our system). Exacerbation may be due to increased etOH intake vs pna vs afib vs salt indiscretion. Troponins negative, EKG shows LBBB but negative for Sgarbossa's criteria. During the course of hospitalization was diuresed effectively with 40mg IV Lasix, lost ~6kg, discharge weight 84.2kg. Discharged on torsemide 20mg daily as 40mg daily was too much. Decreased lisinopril to 5mg daily because of border line low BP (100s-110s systolic) and metoprolol XL 100mg daily instead of the atenolol. Stopped home HCTZ/triamterene on d/c. # Toxometabolic encephalopathy: Patient presented with confusion per wife in the setting of unintentional overdose in the setting of ambien/wine/nyquil. Appears improved. # Cough/RLL infiltrate: Plus coarse rhonchi in R lung base on exam, consistent with PNA. DDx includes pneumonia vs aspiration which patient is high risk for with alcoholism and unintentional medication overdose. Negative legionella. Treated with CTX/azithro x5 days # Atrial fibrillation on Xarelto: s/p ablation (___), pt with recent ZioPatch ___ to assess afib burden and rates when in Afib given worsening EF and concern that afib/tachycardia may be causing CM. ZioPatch results show Afib throughout, average ventricular rate 115/min, IVCD, no pauses. Large amount (10%) of ventricular ectopy, mostly VPBs. No patient events. Switched atenolol to metoprolol and continued on rivaroxaban. # Elevated INR: 5.8. Likely due to combination of poor PO intake, xarelto. Consider cirrhosis from ETOH as well. Improved with vitK, likely nutritional in setting of alcohol abuse. At discharge under 2 # ___: creatinine 1.5 from 1.1 baseline. Improved with mild diuresis. Possibly combination of fluid overload plus medication overdose. # Alcohol abuse: Patient with history of etOH abuse, has been strongly advised by MDs to discontinue etOH use. Patient has been poorly compliant and drinks about ___ drinks/day for ___ years. On CIWA for several days in the hospital but did not score. Given thiamine/folate/MVI and consulted SW. Discharged with rehab f/u. #chronic tophaceous gout: on colchicine but not allopurinol. Uric acid level 7.5, tophi apparent by b/l elbows L>R, started allopurinol ___ daily #OSA: continued CPAP while in house #HTN: restarting lisinopril at 5 mg # GERD: continued PPI #Elevated transaminases: unclear etiology. Negative hepatitis serologies, non-alcoholic pattern (ALT >AST). RUQ U/S normal. Trending down before discharge. Recommend f/u at outpatient visit. #CAD prevention: no CAD seen on most recent cath (___). Continue asa, statin Transitional issues: -Given conduction dx, PVCs, consider ICD and/or resynchronization -switched atenolol to metoprolol -decreased lisinopril from 12.5mg to 5mg daily -added torsemide 20mg daily -started allopurinol for chronic tophaceous gout, should recheck uric acid in 6 weeks for goal <4 -recheck transaminases at outpatient f/u
summary: You were admitted because of confusion that was likely related to an unintentional overdose of medications. We also found, however, that you likely had pneumonia and were fluid overloaded from your heart failure. We gave you IV medications to remove excess fluid that is distributed in the lungs and legs as a result of decreased heart muscle function. You will need to take a medication called torsemide every day that will keep the fluid from re-accumulating in the lungs and legs. We are also changing some of your other medications to optimize your regimen for heart failure. Atenolol has been changed to Metoprolol. Triamterene-HCTZ is discontinued. Allopurinol has been started for gout. The dose of lisinopril has been decreased.

text: Brief Hospital Course: #. SOB/cough: The patient reports he has had productive cough for the past 2 weeks prior to admission, which started during his recent admission. Cough is worse when lying flat and he has had to sleep sitting up. At baseline he sleeps on two pillows. He was treated for possible pneumonia/bronchitis during his previous admission. CXR on admission was notable for bibasilar atelectasis. No leukocytosis. Afebrile. Breath sounds are coarse on exam and has significant ___ edema. His SOB and cough were felt to be most likely secondary to a CHF exacerbation, despite the fact that he had a recent TTE in ___ that was essentially normal. His BNP was elevated to 656. He was treated with albuterol and atrovent nebulizers, and IV lasix. A TTE was performed and revealed no significant change from prior (no wall motion abnormalities, no significant valvular disease, and likely normal EF though suboptimal study). He was not discharged on standing diuretics. . #. LLE swelling: The patient noted onset of LLE swelling on the morning of admission. He had swelling in his RLE as well, but not as much. This swelling is most likely secondary to volume overload. He was evaluated by podiatry in the emergency room who felt there was no evidence for infection. The wound site was healing well with granulation tissue and no purulence or malodor. He likely has poor lympatic drainage from his multiple surgeries at his left foot resulting in his left leg swelling significantly more than the right in the setting of congestive heart failure exacerbation. He was continued on vancomycin 1g every 12 hours per podiatry to be completed at rehab after a total of 4 week course on ___. He was fitted for a cloth pneumatic boot while in the hospital. He does need to go to orthopedics clinic on ___ to have a more permanent bi-valve cast placed. . #. Acute renal failure: On admission, his creatinine was 1.5. His baseline creatinine appears to be 1.1 however during his last admission, his creatinine ranged 1.3-1.5. He may have had mild acute renal failure. On day 4, his creatinine came down to 1.2, without any intervention. He was diuresed with IV lasix as above. . #. Anemia: His hematocrit ranged from ___. Baseline appears to be in the low ___, however was 26.5 on discharge on ___ so is currently stable. Previous work-up consistent with iron deficiency anemia. The patient will likely need outpatient colonoscopy to look for occult bleeding source. . #. CAD: H/o LAD stent in ___. No chest pain currently. Has ___ degree AV nodal conduction delay, but this is seen on prior EKG. One set CE negative in the ED. He was continued on ASA, BB. . #. DM2: He was continued on his outpatient lantus regimen and a regular insulin sliding scale. . #. HTN: He was continued on his outpatient Imdur, Norvasc, metoprolol. . #. GERD: He was continued on his outpatient PPI. . #. Code: He was full code for this admission.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with left leg swelling and cough. You had an ultrasound of your left leg which showed no evidence of a blood clot. You were also followed by the podiatry team while you were in the hospital and fitted with a special boot to help stablize your ankle. You will need to get a more permanent cast placed on your left leg. You should get this done on ___ in the ___ clinic on the second floor of the ___ building. You were also treated for a flare of your congestive heart failure (or retention of fluid) with diuretics. You had an echocardiogram which showed normal pumping function of your heart.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old with known CAD, s/p CABG, multiple MI's, multiple PCI's, HTN, HL, DM, cardiomyopathy (with LVEF ___, OSA, hypothyroidism, TIA, s/p dual-chamber ICD in ___ with chest pain for past 4 months presents for elective cardiac cath, s/p DES to SVG-LAD graft, with recent ICD firing. . # Rhythm: The patient was in a normal sinus rhythm during admission. Interrogation of the ICD when he presented to the ED revealed that the patient had undergone an episode of VT that failed ATPx3 and successfully resolved with 25J CV. His metoprolol was increased to 75mg daily upon discharge. He did not have any electrolyte disturbances, heart failure, or ICD breaks that might otherwise cause disturbances to his rhythm. # Coronary artery disease: Patient denied any chest pain or signs or symptoms consistent with CAD. He has an extensive CAD history with CABG, multiple MIs, multiple PCI. He was ruled out for MI, and was continued on his home ASA 325 mg PO daily, Plavix 75 mg Daily, and Lisinopril 5 mg PO daily. His Metoprolol Succinate was increased to 75 mg Daily on discharge. # Systolic congestive heart failure: The patient has a history of CHF with most recent echo (___) showing EF of 25%. The patient has ICD placement to presvent SCD, but did not have any signs or symptoms of heart failure currently. His home lisinopril and furosemide at 80 mg daily were continued. His digoxin was likewise continued, and a digoxin level revealed a therapeutic level. His Metoprolol Succinate was increased to 75 mg Daily on discharge. # Primary Hyperparathyroidism: On admission, the patient's calcium was elevated, and phosphorus was low, suggestive of hyperparathyroidism. The patient's PTH level as checked and foudn to be elevated in the 100s. Endocrine was consulted, and indicated that there were no clear indications for parathyroidectomy (age not < ___, Ca level not 1 mg/dl above upper limit of nl, no osteoporosis history, CrCl >60). On discharge, they recommended PCP ___ for ___ to evaluate for osteoporosis, in addition to a Vitamin D level, a well as a 24 h urinary calcium level. Dr. ___ patient's endocrinologist, was notified by the endocrine fellow of these findings. The team also suggesting having annual calcium and renal function checked to determine if he develops indication for surgery, with ___ q ___ years. # Hypothyroidism: Thyroid functions repeated in house were within normal limits. The patient was continued on his home dose of 25 mcg levothyroxine daily
summary: You were admitted because your ICD had fired. We admitted you to the hospital for further monitoring; your chest x-ray was clear, and your electrolytes were all within normal limits.
